"You're early," Des said, not a question, a statement of inconvenience. "Yeah." He limped past with a trolley, something covered, something I didn't look at. I followed him to the chapel, helped him arrange chairs, the left side not the right, he'd shown me, I'd forgotten, he didn't say anything, just moved them, the silence worse than correction. I took out the key. Held it in my palm where he could see if he looked, if he chose to look, if he cared enough. "Storage locker," I said. "Yale. Small. Bus station, maybe?" Des stopped arranging chairs. He looked at the key, not at me, the way he looks at bodies, practical, assessing. He took it, held it to the light from the chapel's high windows, the grey Sheffield light that makes everything look like a photograph from the seventies. "Demolished," he said. "Bus station lockers. Last month." He handed the key back, his fingers dry, slightly rough, the fingers of someone who handles things, who doesn't ask what they are. "Salvage yard. Grenoside way. They'd have kept them, maybe. Metal." I nodded like this was helpful, like I knew what to do with this information, like I had a plan beyond following the key to wherever it led, beyond needing to do something with my hands, my mouth, my broken not-crying self. "Your funeral's at two," Des said. "Mr. Henderson. Lilies. You ordered lilies." "Alstroemeria," I said, the word from yesterday, Des's word, not mine. He looked at me then. Direct, longer than comfortable, the same look as Mrs. Chen, as the security guard, the seeing-through that I can't distinguish from seeing-properly. "Alstroemeria," he repeated. "Right." I went to the salvage yard after my shift, the key burning in my pocket, the photograph still bent in my other pocket, the building still unrecognized, the summer still unremembered. The yard was open, nobody at the gate, the kind of place that runs on trust or indifference or both. I walked between stacks of metal, old radiators, pipes, things I couldn't identify, the industrial archaeology of a city that used to make things. The lockers were in a corner, twenty or thirty of them, stacked like coffins, like the crematorium's back room where they keep the urns before collection. I found the numbers, matched the key, third try, the lock stiff, my hands cold, stupid, everything stupid. Inside: a notebook. Izzy's handwriting, not coded, not mysterious, just abbreviated, boring, the handwriting of someone managing something tedious. "Blood test 9am." "Hb low." "Transfusion?" And dates, months of dates, appointments I didn't know about, couldn't have known about, because Izzy told me they were her mother's appointments, because I believed her, because I wanted to believe her, because not believing would have required asking, pushing, being the kind of friend who sees through performance instead of resenting it. I stopped reading. Put the notebook back. Took it again. The pages were thin, medical, the kind you get from hospitals, from waiting rooms, from the NHS that runs on paper and patience and people not making a fuss. Izzy had made notes like she made everything, organized, controlled, the performance of competence even in illness, especially in illness, especially in the thing she wouldn't name to me. A second key at the back of the locker. Smaller, different, no initials, no stickers, no indication. I held both keys, the first useless now, the second opening something I didn't know, somewhere I couldn't find, the search continuing because stopping was impossible, because stopping meant sitting with what I'd found, what I hadn't found, the fact of Izzy's body failing her while I ate her terrible lentil stew and said it was nice and threw it away later. I put the notebook in my bag. Left the first key in the locker, couldn't bear to carry it, couldn't bear to throw it away. Walked out of the salvage yard without seeing anyone, without being seen, or being seen and not mattering, the same thing. On the bus home, the 52 finally, I opened the notebook again. "Hb low." I googled on my phone, the screen still flickering from the water damage, the emojis still there, still unanswered. Hemoglobin. Anemia, maybe. Something worse. The word "aplastic" appeared, I didn't click, couldn't, the bus moving through streets I knew too well, Hillsborough, home, the same streets, the same houses, the same life continuing around this hole where Izzy had been. My mother was home, early shift, something wrong with her voice when she called up the stairs. I put the notebook under my mattress without reading more, without facing more, the cowardice I can't stop performing. Came down. She'd made pasta, the same three meals in rotation, slightly wrong, slightly burned, the sauce from a jar she claimed was homemade. "Good day?" "Yeah." The same conversation. The same lie. "Study?" "Yeah." We ate in silence that was not comfortable, that was never comfortable, that was the best we could manage. I thought about telling her. The notebook, the blood tests, the appointments Izzy said were her mother's. I opened my mouth. Nothing came out. What would I say, my dead friend was sick, my dead friend lied, my dead friend is still lying from beyond the grave or not beyond, from scheduled messages and hidden notebooks and the everything is too much that I still can't read? I didn't tell her. I finished the pasta. I washed up, her way, wrong. I went upstairs. I lay on my bed. I took out the notebook. I read "Hb low" again. I read "Transfusion?" with the question mark, Izzy's question mark, the uncertainty she allowed herself in private, that she never showed me, that I never asked to see. The second key sat on my windowsill, catching the streetlight. I didn't try it in anything. I knew it wouldn't fit our doors, our shed, our lives. It opened something I hadn't found yet, somewhere Izzy had been without me, something she carried alone, the weight I didn't help with because I didn't know, because she didn't tell me, because I didn't ask, because I didn't ask, because I didn't ask. I counted my breaths. I lost count. I started again. The notebook smelled of the locker, of metal, of something else, something I couldn't name, Izzy's smell almost, almost, not quite, the way everything about her is almost, close enough to hurt, wrong when I touch it. I didn't sleep. This is not new. But this night was different. This night I had something of hers, something she hadn't meant for me or had meant for me, I couldn't tell, would never be able to tell, the ambiguity permanent, the not-knowing the point, the not-knowing the grief, the not-knowing everything, everything, everything.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong execution of the plan, demonstrating genuine craft in several areas while having some minor weaknesses worth examining. **Voice and Character** Rosa's voice is consistently rendered. The chapter captures her self-aware paralysis—the "don't-fuss thing that kills us," the "British thing, the Sheffield thing"—without over-explaining it. The internal monologue moves between sharp observation and deliberate flatness in ways that feel earned rather than performed. The repetition of "because I didn't ask, because I didn't ask, because I didn't ask" at the chapter's close is one of the stronger moments: compulsive, rhythmically appropriate, emotionally honest without being melodramatic. The characterization of Mrs. Chen is excellent. Her line "You didn't even like her. Not really. I saw. I saw how you waited for her to finish, waited to go home. Don't cry now. Don't you dare cry now." is devastating and specific, and Rosa's response—not crying, not capable of crying—is consistent with the character profile and the broader emotional architecture of the story. Des is handled well. He's not wise, not a mentor, just present and practical. The key identification scene is efficient and believable. His "Alstroemeria" callback is a small, well-placed moment of dry comedy that doesn't overstay its welcome. **Plot Execution** The chapter follows its plan closely: the failed key at Izzy's house, Mrs. Chen's confrontation, Des identifying the locker type, the salvage yard, the notebook with medical abbreviations, the second key. The discovery of "Hb low" and "Transfusion?" is handled with appropriate restraint—Rosa doesn't decode it immediately, googles it on the bus, stops before "aplastic," the cowardice of not-knowing preserved. This is faithful to the plan's instruction that Rosa "stops reading" and "puts the notebook back. Takes it again." The flashback fragment (Izzy's lentil stew, the lie of "it was nice") is integrated into the present-tense narration rather than set apart as a discrete flashback, which is a slight deviation from the plan but arguably more effective—it emerges naturally from Rosa's guilt rather than as a formal interruption. **Prose Quality** The prose is largely clean and controlled. Sentence fragments are used appropriately for panic and avoidance. The run-on tendency appears in the guilt passages, which is consistent with the stated stylistic intention. There are specific, grounding details: the 52 bus, the vending machine with crisps from 2019, the Yale key, the NHS paper. These work. Some moments tip toward over-explanation. "The cowardice I can't stop performing" is slightly too self-aware, too articulate for a character who is supposed to be "stupid with grief" sometimes. Similarly, "the not-knowing the point, the not-knowing the grief, the not-knowing everything, everything, everything" is the chapter's weakest moment—it names the theme too directly, tells us what the story is doing rather than letting it do it. The plan explicitly warned against thematic overdetermination, and this final paragraph edges toward it. **Dialogue** The dialogue is sparse and mostly effective. Mrs. Chen's confrontation is the strongest exchange. Des's lines are appropriately terse. The dialogue "misses" in the way the plan intended—Rosa and Des talk past each other, the conversation about the key is practical rather than emotional. The weakness is that there isn't much dialogue to assess, and what exists is competent rather than exceptional. **Tone** The darkly comic elements are present but understated in this chapter—the vending machine, the wrong flowers (alstroemeria), the 52 bus not coming. The comedy doesn't tip into whimsy. The chapter is predominantly in the grief register, which is appropriate given its position in the arc (discovery of the medical notebook is a significant emotional beat). The burned pasta dinner scene is quietly effective. **Weaknesses** The final paragraph is the main structural weakness—it over-explains the emotional significance of what has just happened. "The not-knowing the point, the not-knowing the grief" is the kind of thematic statement the plan explicitly wanted to avoid. The chapter would be stronger ending on the physical detail of the notebook's smell, or the second key on the windowsill, rather than the abstract summation. The line "the industrial archaeology of a city that used to make things" is slightly too writerly for Rosa's voice—it sounds like an author's observation rather than a character's. Overall, this is a well-executed chapter that honors the plan, maintains character consistency, and achieves genuine emotional weight without sentimentality. The flaws are real but minor. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I do nothing about the second key for two days. This is not strategy. This is not dramatic pause. This is me, Rosa, seventeen, unable to carry the weight of what I've found, letting it sit on my windowsill where I don't have to touch it, where I can pretend it doesn't exist while checking it every seventeen minutes. The notebook stays under my mattress. I don't read further. I know what I know and I don't want to know more, the cowardice I perform like a second skin, like the black shoes I polish badly, like everything I do slightly wrong because doing it right would require being present, being here, being someone who can face things. Monday I go to school. Technically study leave, technically finished, but there's a leavers' event I forgot to avoid, a gathering in the common room with cheap squash and speeches from people I don't know. I sit in the corner. Someone talks about futures, university, the exciting things ahead. I think about the crematorium, the conveyor belt, the way Des hums without knowing. I think about Izzy, who had no future, who had futures she didn't share, who scheduled emojis instead of telling me she was dying. I leave early. Walk to the bus stop. The 52 doesn't come. I walk home through streets that are the same streets, the same grey, the same March that won't become April properly, just more of this, more of everything. My mother is at work. The house is the particular empty that feels accusatory, the television off, the silence worse than her talk show noise. I make tea, my way, don't drink it. Lie on the sofa. Count ceiling cracks. There are eleven. I count them three times, getting eleven each time, which should be reassuring but isn't. I should study. I should do something. I should call someone, the numbers in my phone of people who knew Izzy, who knew me-with-Izzy, who might want to talk about her or might not, who might know something I don't or might know less, who might ask questions I can't answer. I do none of this. I watch the light change on the ceiling. I think about the second key. I don't move to pick it up. Tuesday I work at the crematorium. A funeral for someone named Henderson, the one Des mentioned, the lilies I ordered wrong, the alstroemeria that nobody noticed except me. I put out hymn sheets. I arrange flowers. I say "sorry for your loss" to people who don't look at me, who see through me like I'm not there, which I almost am not, which is how I prefer it. The funeral goes wrong. Not dramatically. Quietly, stupidly, my fault. I put the wrong name on the order of service. Not Henderson. Someone else, a name from last week, a funeral I don't remember specifically but my fingers do, the repetition, the same forms, the same names blurring into each other. Nobody notices until the end, until a daughter, someone's daughter, points at the booklet, the wrong name, her father's service with someone else's life summarized inside. Des handles it. He always handles it, the practical man, the man who doesn't ask. He gives out corrected booklets, apologies without explanation, the institutional smoothing-over that makes things technically alright without making them right. He doesn't look at me. This is worse than looking. After, in the back, he says: "Mistake." "I know." "Don't make it again." "I won't." This is not forgiveness. This is not teaching. This is Des performing his function, the maintenance of order, the keeping-going that he does, that I fail at, that I will continue failing at because the notebook under my mattress has made everything else unimportant, has made my own incompetence feel deserved, appropriate, the punishment for not asking, not seeing, not being the friend I thought I was. I go to a party I don't want to attend. This is not in the plan, not in any plan, just something that happens because Amy from biology texts me, because I say yes without thinking, because not going would require explaining, because going requires nothing, just showing up, just being present in body while absent in everything else. It's in a house I don't know, someone's parents away, the usual thing, the music too loud, the lights too bright, everything designed to make you feel like you're having fun without requiring you to actually feel anything. I drink warm lager from a cup someone else used, not thinking about germs, not thinking about anything, the alcohol hitting my empty stomach like a promise it can't keep. Amy talks to me. I don't listen properly. She says something about Izzy, I think, something sympathetic, something that uses the past tense in a way that feels wrong, violent, the grammar of death that I haven't accepted, that I perform acceptance of without believing. I nod. I say "yeah" at the wrong place. She looks at me, the look I've been getting, the seeing-through, and moves away, finds someone easier, someone present, someone capable of the performance I can no longer manage. I kiss someone. This is not a decision. This is momentum, the same momentum that brought me to the reservoir, to the salvage yard, to the notebook under my mattress. Someone, I don't know who, don't remember their name, their face a blur of acne and enthusiasm. They taste of the same lager, of something sweet underneath, of youth and wanting and the desperation to connect that I feel without being able to return. Their hand on my waist. My hand on their shoulder. The kiss going nowhere, meaning nothing, feeling like an autopsy of something I used to understand. I pull away. They say something. I don't answer. I find the bathroom, lock the door, sit on the closed toilet. Not crying. Still not crying. The broken thing in me that doesn't know how. I look at my phone. The emojis still there. No new messages. I check Izzy's social media again, the frozen posts, the comments from strangers, the memorial that isn't her, that never was her, that is a performance of grief by people who didn't know her well enough to know she would have hated it. I throw up. Not from alcohol. From something else, something the alcohol uncovered, the emptiness I've been living in, the hunger I don't feed, the grief I don't feel properly, the everything is too much that Izzy sent and I still can't read. It's mostly liquid, acid, the nothing I've been consuming. I flush. I don't look in the mirror. I know what I'll see. I walk home. It's far. I don't care. The night is cold, March cold that gets into your bones, the Sheffield wind that comes from nowhere and goes nowhere, just circles, just this. I pass the reservoir, don't stop, can't stop, won't stop, the water brown in darkness, the things floating I don't identify. My mother is awake when I get in. Sitting in the kitchen, the television off, a mug of something in her hands that isn't tea. "You're back then." "Yeah." She looks at me. The look I can't read, that I've never been able to read, that might be concern or might be disappointment or might be nothing, just her face, just her tiredness, just the climate she lives in that I can't distinguish from weather. "Food if you want. It's not—" she gestures at the stove, at something burned, something slightly wrong, her three meals in rotation "—it's whatever it is." "I'm not hungry." "You're never hungry." Not accusatory. Statement. Fact. Something she's noticed without mentioning, the way she notices everything without mentioning, the accumulation of observations that never becomes conversation. I snap. This is not planned. This is not dramatic. This is the pressure finding the weakest point, the crack in the dam, the everything I've been not-saying since the emojis arrived, since before, since always. "What do you want me to say? What do you want? I'm fine. I'm always fine. That's what you want, isn't it? Fine, fine, everything fine, don't make a fuss, don't—" I stop. She's looking at me. The same look. The seeing-through. And something else, something I don't recognize, something that might be hurt or might be recognition or might be her own everything she's not-saying, her own notebook under her mattress, her own key to something she never found. "Rosa—" I don't let her finish. Can't let her finish. Whatever she says will be wrong, will require response, will crack the performance further, will make me feel things I can't afford to feel, not now, not with the emojis waiting, the notebook waiting, the second key on my windowsill waiting. I go upstairs. Close my door. Not loudly. Not quietly. Just closed. I lie on my bed. I count my breaths. I lose count. I start again. I hear her downstairs, moving around, the clink of her mug in the sink, the television going on, the talk show noise she sleeps through, the life continuing around this hole where I am, where Izzy was, where everything is too much, too much, too much. I don't sleep. This is not new. But this night is different. This night I have the memory of kissing someone whose name I forgot, of throwing up in a stranger's bathroom, of snapping at my mother who didn't deserve it, who never deserves it, who is trying in her way, her wrong way, her slightly-burned-dinner way. This night I have the second key on my windowsill, still untouched, still opening something I haven't found, and the knowledge that I'm not looking, that I'm avoiding, that the everything is too much includes me, my failures, my cowardice, my inability to be the person Izzy needed, the person I need myself to be. I check my phone. The emojis. Still there. Still unanswered. I type a reply, delete it, type again, delete again. What would I say, what do you say, hey Izzy, found your notebook, found your lies, found the appointments you said were your mother's, found the blood tests, found everything you didn't want me to find, found I wasn't worth telling, found— I don't send anything. I put the phone down. I count ceiling cracks. Eleven. I count them again. Eleven. The number doesn't change. The cracks don't widen. The night doesn't end, just continues, just more of this, more of everything, the key waiting, the notebook waiting, the grief waiting, me not ready, never ready, always not ready, always wrong.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 ("Nothing") is a strong piece of writing that largely succeeds at its stated aims: a chapter that "falls apart," goes nowhere, and captures grief as stasis rather than progress. Let me assess it carefully across the relevant dimensions. STRENGTHS: The voice is remarkably consistent and well-calibrated. Rosa's interior monologue achieves the planned stylistic markers: fragments for panic, run-ons for avoidance, flat declaratives that land. "I do nothing about the second key for two days. This is not strategy. This is not dramatic pause." This opening is excellent—it pre-empts the reader's interpretation, which is itself a character move, Rosa defending herself against a judgment she anticipates. The self-awareness without self-knowledge is precisely right. The crematorium mistake (wrong name on the order of service) is handled with real skill. The daughter noticing, Des's minimal response, the institutional smoothing-over—this is exactly the "grief as physical comedy that sometimes tips into actual pain" the plan calls for. "This is not forgiveness. This is not teaching. This is Des performing his function" is a clean, accurate observation that doesn't overreach. The party sequence works. The kiss is rendered with appropriate flatness—"an autopsy of something I used to understand" is one of the chapter's better metaphors, genuinely earned rather than decorative. The vomiting scene is handled with the right restraint: "Not from alcohol. From something else." The chapter doesn't over-explain. The mother scene is the chapter's most complex moment. Linda's dialogue is appropriately wrong, appropriately insufficient: "You're never hungry" as observation rather than accusation. Rosa's snap and retreat is psychologically accurate. The detail of Linda's "seeing-through" look that might be hurt or recognition—the chapter wisely refuses to resolve it. The ending—counting ceiling cracks, eleven, the number not changing—is effective. It's not a poetic flourish; it's a compulsive behavior (established in the character profile) deployed at the right moment. WEAKNESSES: The chapter occasionally over-explains its own emotional logic. "The cowardice I perform like a second skin, like the black shoes I polish badly, like everything I do slightly wrong because doing it right would require being present" is a run-on that works structurally but the simile chain becomes slightly labored by the third element. The plan called for "lists as failed coping mechanism—things that don't add up to control," and this is achieved, but sometimes the lists tip into the narrator explaining her own symbolism rather than enacting it. "The grammar of death that I haven't accepted" is a slightly too-literary formulation for Rosa's voice as established. It's the kind of phrase that sounds like a writer rather than a seventeen-year-old from Hillsborough. The flashback fragment—the last time she saw Izzy, "I'm so tired" / "Same"—is present in the plan but absent from the chapter as written. This is a notable omission. The plan explicitly called for this flashback, and its absence means the chapter lacks the structural counterpoint that was intended. This is a fidelity-to-plan issue. The chapter is slightly repetitive in its self-flagellation. Rosa cataloguing her failures—not seeing, not asking, not being present—becomes a list that circles without deepening. The plan called for "some chapters fall apart, some go nowhere," which this achieves, but there's a risk that the going-nowhere becomes its own kind of performance. The phrase "the everything is too much" appears twice, which reads as intentional compulsive repetition, but the third instance ("the everything is too much includes me") slightly over-deploys it. The dialogue is sparse but functional. Des's two-line exchange is perfect. Linda's dialogue is appropriately insufficient. The chapter wisely doesn't give Rosa much dialogue because she's not talking to anyone, which is the point. OVERALL ASSESSMENT: This is a competent-to-strong chapter that achieves most of its aims. The voice is consistent, the emotional logic is sound, the restraint is mostly maintained. The missing flashback is a real gap. Some moments of over-explanation and slightly-too-literary phrasing prevent it from being excellent. The chapter successfully "goes nowhere" in a way that feels intentional rather than slack, which is genuinely difficult to pull off. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 11 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 12 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I use the second key on Wednesday. Not because I'm ready. Because I'm not ready for anything else, because the alternative is another day of lying on my bed counting cracks, another day of my mother's careful not-asking, another day of the emojis burning on my phone while I burn with them. The key fits a hospital locker, the hematology clinic at the Northern General, the place Izzy went for appointments she said were her mother's. I walk the route from the bus stop, past the maternity wing, past the smokers in dressing gowns, past the everything of a hospital that keeps going, keeps treating, keeps failing, keeps trying. The waiting room is the same as every NHS waiting room, the same blue chairs, the same posters about symptoms you should already have reported, the same fluorescent hum that makes everyone look ill even if they're not. I sit where Izzy sat, I tell myself, though I don't know where she sat, which chair, whether she read the magazines or stared at her phone or talked to strangers the way she sometimes did, performing extroversion, collecting people she wouldn't keep. A nurse mistakes me for a patient. She has a clipboard, a name badge I don't read, the efficiency of someone who has seen too many people in one day to see any of them properly. "Rosa Vance?" I almost say yes. The reflex, the wanting to be seen, to be someone who belongs here, who has a reason to be here beyond following dead girl's keys. I shake my head. "Waiting for someone." She looks at me. The look. The seeing-through. But she's too tired, too busy, has too many actual patients to pursue whatever wrongness I represent. "There's a chapel," she says, not kindly, not unkindly, just information. "Down the corridor, left. If you need it." I don't need it. I go anyway. The chapel is small, interfaith, the kind of place that tries to be everything to everyone and ends up being nothing to anyone. Plastic Jesus on one wall. A Buddha I don't look at properly. A carpet that smells of disinfectant and desperation, the particular smell of places where people come when they have nowhere else, when the medical has failed or is failing or hasn't started. I sit on a chair that isn't a pew, that doesn't know what it is. I look at the plastic Jesus. I don't believe in him, don't not believe, just don't have the energy for belief or its absence, the performance of certainty that Izzy could manage, that everyone seems to manage except me. Behind Jesus, a prayer card. Tucked, hidden, not obviously placed. I reach past, the plastic cold, slightly greasy from other hands, other desperations. The card is standard, the kind you get in packs, Jesus's face blurred from cheap printing. On the back, an address, Izzy's writing, the sharp letters she learned later, not the round ones from the photograph, the evolution of her handwriting an archaeology I can read without understanding. I put the card in my pocket. Sit back down. The chair creaks. The fluorescent hum continues. Someone coughs in the corridor, the smoker's cough, the one my mother has, the one that means nothing or everything. I don't pray. I don't know how. I sit in the not-praying, the not-knowing, the everything is too much that the emojis represent, that this address represents, that Izzy's hidden life represents, the life she lived without me, that I didn't see, that I didn't ask to see. In the corridor, I see a girl about Izzy's age, bald, with a mother who looks like mine. Same tiredness, same careful not-touching, same performance of normal in a place that makes normal impossible. The girl has a cannula in her hand, the tape peeling, nobody has fixed it, the small neglects that accumulate in hospitals, in lives, in everything. She looks at me. I look away. I am not what she needs. I am not what anyone needs. I go home. The bus comes, which feels wrong, which feels like a mistake, the 52 actually arriving, actually taking me somewhere, the universe's indifference momentarily suspended. I sit upstairs, front seat, the address in my hand, not looking at it, looking at the streets instead, the same streets, the same grey, the same Sheffield that Izzy moved through without me knowing, that she mapped with appointments and hidden cards and keys to lockers I didn't know existed. My mother is at work. The house is empty, the television off, the silence not accusatory now, just empty, just there. I make tea, my way, drink it cold without noticing. I look at the address. A flat in Nether Edge, a building I don't know, somewhere Izzy went without me, with someone else, for something I can't imagine. I don't go. Not today. I put the address on my windowsill next to the second key, now used, now meaningless, now part of the accumulation of objects that don't add up to understanding. I lie on my bed. I count ceiling cracks. Eleven. I count them again. Eleven. I sleep, finally, accidentally, the sleep of exhaustion, the sleep that comes without invitation. I dream of Izzy, or someone like Izzy, swimming in brown water, her face painted like the photograph, tiger stripes, butterfly blue, the two looks merged, the child and the dead girl, the everything she was and wasn't. She doesn't see me. She swims away. I can't follow. I wake with my throat hurting, as if I've been shouting, though I haven't, though I don't, though the not-shouting is part of the performance, the everything is fine, the don't make a fuss. Thursday I don't go to the address. I work at the crematorium. Des is normal, which is a kindness, which I don't recognize as kindness until later, until I'm lying in bed counting cracks again, eleven, always eleven. A funeral, someone I don't know, the same hymns, the same curtains, the same conveyor belt sound. I put out chairs. I don't make mistakes. This is worse than making them, the competence that feels like betrayal, like moving on, like being the person who can continue while Izzy can't, while Izzy stopped, while Izzy scheduled emojis and hid keys and wrote "Hb low" in notebooks under mattresses. After work, I walk. Not toward the address. Away from it. Through the city centre, past the shops closing, past the buskers, past the everything of Sheffield continuing without Izzy, without me properly, the me that is here but not present, that moves through without touching, without being touched. I find myself at the bus station, the demolished one, the salvage yard where the lockers were. It's closed, evening, nobody there. I stand at the fence, looking through, the stacks of metal dark shapes against darker sky. I think about the notebook, still under my mattress. I think about reading further, about the pages I didn't turn, the information I didn't absorb, the everything Izzy recorded that I can't bear to know. I go home. My mother is awake, unusual, sitting in the kitchen with the television off, the mug in her hands that isn't tea. "You're back then." "Yeah." She looks at me. The look. But something else tonight, something I don't recognize, or do recognize but can't name, the look of someone who has also lost something, who is also not-crying, who also doesn't know how. "Rosa," she says. My name. Just my name. Like it means something. Like she's trying to make it mean something. I don't answer. I can't answer. Whatever I say will be wrong, will open something, will require more than I have. I go upstairs. Close my door. Lie on my bed. The address on my windowsill catches the streetlight. I pick it up. I look at it. I don't go. I don't not-go. I exist in the not-deciding, the not-moving, the everything is too much that has become my climate, my weather, my only geography. I count my breaths. I lose count. I start again. The notebook under my mattress, the emojis on my phone, the address in my hand, the second key now useless on my windowsill, the accumulation of objects that Izzy left or didn't leave, that mean something or nothing, that I follow because following is easier than stopping, than sitting, than feeling what I need to feel, whatever that is, whatever that would look like, the crying I can't do, the screaming I don't allow, the everything that is too much, too much, too much. I don't sleep. This is not new. But this night is different. This night I have the address, the next step, the continuation of the search that isn't a search, that is just movement, just momentum, just the only way I know to be in the world that has Izzy not in it, that has her everywhere and nowhere, in emojis and keys and notebooks and prayer cards behind plastic Jesus, in the bald girl in the corridor, in my mother's look that I can't read, in my own broken not-crying self that continues, somehow, impossibly, wrong, always wrong, always here.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter represents a genuinely accomplished piece of YA literary fiction that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me work through its strengths and weaknesses systematically. VOICE AND INTERIORITY: The chapter's greatest strength is Rosa's voice, which maintains the planned stylistic markers with real discipline. The sentence fragments during panic ("Not because I'm ready. Because I'm not ready for anything else") and the run-ons during avoidance are deployed with genuine craft rather than mechanical application. The repetition of "too much, too much, too much" at the end risks tipping into self-parody but is earned by the accumulation preceding it. The voice is consistently unreliable in productive ways—Rosa tells us she sits "where Izzy sat" then immediately undercuts it ("though I don't know where she sat"), which is exactly the kind of self-aware unreliability the plan called for. CHARACTERIZATION: Rosa is rendered with real nuance here. Her near-reflex to say "yes" to the nurse who calls her name is a small, devastating detail—the desire to belong somewhere, to have a legitimate reason to be in the space Izzy occupied. The observation about the bald girl and her mother ("I am not what she needs. I am not what anyone needs") is genuinely painful without being overwrought. The counting of ceiling cracks (eleven, always eleven) is consistent with the character profile and functions as a behavioral tic rather than a symbol, which is the right choice. PLOT EXECUTION: The chapter follows its plan closely—Rosa uses the second key, finds the hospital locker, sits in the waiting room, is mistaken for a patient, finds the chapel, discovers the prayer card with an address. The plan's note that she goes home without looking at the address is honored. The Thursday interlude (crematorium, walking, bus station) adds texture without padding excessively. The mother scene at the end is brief but effective. WEAKNESSES: The chapter's main vulnerability is a tendency toward repetitive formulation. The phrase "the everything of" appears multiple times ("the everything of a hospital," "the everything she was and wasn't," "the everything that is too much") and while it's a distinctive Rosa-ism, it becomes a verbal tic that starts to feel like a stylistic crutch rather than a character trait. Similarly, the accumulation of "the not-crying, the not-knowing, the everything is too much" constructions in the final paragraphs becomes slightly numbing—the technique is sound but overused within a single chapter. The dream sequence is the weakest passage. "I dream of Izzy, or someone like Izzy, swimming in brown water, her face painted like the photograph, tiger stripes, butterfly blue" is competent but feels like the most conventionally literary moment in an otherwise unconventional chapter—it's the kind of dream-as-symbol that the plan explicitly wanted to avoid. The "child and the dead girl, the everything she was and wasn't" framing is too neat. DIALOGUE: There is very little dialogue, which is appropriate for this chapter. The nurse exchange is functional and real. The mother's "Rosa" at the end—just the name—is genuinely effective, a small moment that carries weight without explanation. SHOW VS. TELL: The chapter mostly shows rather than tells, but there are moments where Rosa's interior monologue becomes too explanatory: "the life she lived without me, that I didn't see, that I didn't ask to see" tells us what we've already understood from the action. The chapter occasionally over-explains its own emotional logic. TONAL CONSISTENCY: The plan called for "grief as physical comedy that sometimes tips into actual pain without warning" and "institutional death as genuinely absurd." This chapter is almost entirely in the pain register—the comedy is largely absent. The bus arriving ("which feels wrong, which feels like a mistake, the 52 actually arriving, actually taking me somewhere, the universe's indifference momentarily suspended") is the closest thing to the darkly comic register, and it's effective, but the chapter is more uniformly mournful than the plan intended. The crematorium Thursday section is competent but doesn't generate the absurdist texture the plan promised. FORCED POETRY: Some metaphors land cleanly ("the sleep that comes without invitation"), others are slightly strained ("the not-deciding, the not-moving, the everything is too much that has become my climate, my weather, my only geography"). The final paragraph's accumulation of objects and negations is ambitious but slightly overwrought. OVERALL: This is strong literary fiction with a distinctive, disciplined voice. Its weaknesses are real—overuse of certain formulations, a dream sequence that doesn't earn its place, occasional over-explanation—but they don't undermine the chapter's fundamental achievement. It follows the plan faithfully, maintains character consistency, and generates genuine emotional engagement without sentimentality. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 7 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I break into the flat on Friday. This is not a decision. This is momentum, the same momentum that carried me to the reservoir, the salvage yard, the hospital chapel, the only way I know to move, forward into whatever Izzy left, whatever she didn't explain, whatever she thought I could understand without her telling me. The building is in Nether Edge, Victorian, subdivided, being renovated in stops and starts, the scaffolding permanent, the builders appearing and disappearing on no schedule I can determine. I watch from the bus stop opposite. Nobody enters. Nobody leaves. The streetlight flickers, orange, the Sheffield light that makes everyone look ill. The scaffolding gap is obvious, meant for access, not meant for me but accessible to me, my body small enough, desperate enough, wrong enough. I squeeze through. My coat catches again, the same coat, the same rip widening, I don't care, I don't care about anything except the address, the next step, the continuation of not-stopping. The flat is empty. Not empty like cleaned, empty like abandoned, like someone left in a hurry or never fully arrived. A mattress on the floor, stained, the kind of stain you don't examine. A stack of letters on the windowsill, held down by a medical textbook, something about blood disorders, the kind of book that exists in waiting rooms, that nobody reads fully. I pick up the letters. They are from Izzy, I know her handwriting now, the sharp letters, the evolution from the round childish forms, the person she became without me noticing, without me asking, without me being there for the becoming. They are addressed to "C," no other name, no address, unsent, unaddressed in any way that would allow sending. I sit on the mattress. The stain is cold through my jeans. I don't move. I read. The first letter is boring, medical, the kind of thing Izzy would write if she were writing to a doctor, to someone who needed facts. "Blood test 9am, Hb 8.2, lower than last time." The second is angrier. "Mum asked if I'm eating enough. I said yes. I said yes to everything. It's easier." The third: "I saw you at the crematorium today. You were putting out chairs, very precise, very Rosa. I watched from across the road and I couldn't go in. What would I say? I'm dying? I'm already dead? I'm fine?" I stop reading. I can't not read. I continue. The letters get worse. Not worse in information, worse in something else, in the Izzy they reveal, the Izzy who watched me without approaching, who wrote to someone she couldn't name about things she couldn't say to me, who needed and didn't need, who wanted to be seen and couldn't bear being seen. "I want to tell you but you're already carrying too much and you'd try to carry this too and I can't stand being carried." I read this three times. Four. The words don't change. The meaning doesn't clarify. I am carrying too much, I am carrying nothing, I don't know what I carry, what Izzy thought I carried, what she saw in me that made me unapproachable, that made her hide in flats with stained mattresses writing letters to "C" that she never sent. The final letter is different. Shorter. "The results came back and I didn't open them for three hours. I made tea I didn't drink. I watched something on my phone I don't remember. When I finally looked it was what I expected and I felt nothing, then everything, then nothing again." No signature. No "love Izzy." Just the end, the stopping, the everything is too much that the emojis represent, that all of this represents, that I still can't read properly, will never read properly, the ambiguity permanent. I sit on the mattress too long. The light changes, grey to darker grey, March evening coming without announcement. I don't move. I am crying, I realize, finally, the thing I couldn't do, doing itself without my permission, ugly, silent, my face wet, my nose running, no tissue, I wipe it on my sleeve, disgusting, human, present in a way I haven't been. A builder finds me. The door opening, the light from the corridor, his silhouette. I don't flinch. I don't hide the letters. What would be the point, what would ever be the point. "Fuck," he says. Not angry. Surprised. The surprise of finding something human where you expected emptiness. I say: "I'm a prospective tenant." The lie obvious, my face wet, the letters in my hands, the mattress stained, everything wrong, everything visible. He looks at me. The look. But different, this time, not seeing-through but seeing, actually seeing, the discomfort of it, the intrusion. "You alright, love?" I hate "love." I hate the diminutive, the automatic, the way it makes small things smaller. But I don't say this. I cry harder, silently, ugly, the performance of grief that isn't performance, that is finally actual, finally present, finally here. He lets me go. Doesn't call anyone, doesn't report anything, just steps back, lets me pass, the kindness of not-knowing-what-to-do, of choosing inaction over action that might make things worse. I take the letters. All of them. I don't know if I should, don't care if I should, the shoulds abandoned somewhere, the reservoir maybe, the hospital chapel, the flat with the stained mattress and the medical textbook and the letters to "C" that I will never understand, never fully, never finally. On the bus home, the 52 actually coming, actually taking me somewhere, the universe's indifference suspended again, I read the last letter again. "I felt nothing, then everything, then nothing again." This is Izzy. This is the Izzy I didn't know, that I didn't ask to know, that I couldn't bear to know, that I bear now, carrying too much, carrying nothing, not knowing the difference. My mother is awake. The kitchen, the mug, the television off. She looks at me. I look at her. Something passes between us, something I can't name, the recognition of two people who are not-crying, who don't know how, who have learned to perform fine, fine, everything fine. "Rosa," she says. My name. Again. "Izzy was sick," I say. The words out before I can stop them, before I can perform not-saying, not-telling, not-making-a-fuss. "She was sick and she didn't tell me and I didn't ask and she's dead and I didn't ask and I didn't—" I stop. My mother looks at me. The look. The seeing-through. But something else, something that might be her own not-asking, her own not-telling, her own everything she carries that I don't know about, that I've never asked about, the father who left, the life before me, the life with me, the everything is too much that she performs as tiredness, as burned dinners, as television noise. "I'm sorry," she says. Not for Izzy. For something else. For everything else. For the not-asking, the not-telling, the way we are with each other, the way we've always been, the performance of family that never becomes actual, never becomes present, never becomes here. I go upstairs. Close my door. Not loudly. Not quietly. Just closed. I lie on my bed. The letters in my hands, on my chest, the weight of them, the lightness, the everything and nothing they contain. I read them again. All of them. The medical facts, the anger, the watching me at the crematorium, the not-coming-in, the everything she couldn't say, that I couldn't hear, that we failed at together, the friendship that was real and insufficient, present and absent, everything and not-enough. I find the photograph from the geocache box, the bent water-damaged image, the two of us at thirteen, faces painted, the building I didn't recognize. I look at it now, really look, and I see it: the white building, the distinctive roof, the place Izzy took me once, a weekend, her father's conference, we were left alone, we explored, we found this building, some museum, some nothing, some memory that mattered to her enough to hide, to reference, to leave for me to find. I don't remember it properly. I remember being bored, checking my phone, wanting to go back to the hotel, the performance of interest I gave, the same performance I always gave, the same not-being-present that Izzy saw, that she wrote about, that she couldn't bear to approach, that made me unapproachable, that made her hide in flats with stained mattresses writing letters to "C" that she never sent. I cry again. This is new. This is the crying continuing, not the one-time thing I expected, not the breakthrough that leads to healing, to graduation, to better. This is crying that doesn't stop, that I don't control, that comes and goes, that is present in a way I haven't been present, that is here, that is here, that is here. I don't sleep. This is not new. But this night is different. This night I have the letters, the real Izzy, the insufficient Izzy, the Izzy who watched me and couldn't approach, who needed and couldn't need, who wrote "I can't stand being carried" and meant it, who drowned or didn't drown, who scheduled emojis and hid keys and wrote letters and died or stopped or became nothing, everything, nothing again. I count my breaths. I lose count. I don't start again. The counting abandoned, the ceiling cracks uncounted, the eleven becoming nothing, becoming everything, becoming the too much that Izzy sent, that I still can't read, that I will spend my life misreading, that I want to misread because the alternative is worse, the alternative is that she meant nothing, that the emojis were placeholder, that I am following breadcrumbs that lead nowhere because nowhere was all she had, all I have, all any of us have, the nothing, the everything, the too much, the too much, the too much.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 6 is a strong piece of writing that largely succeeds at what it sets out to do, though with some notable weaknesses that prevent it from being exceptional. **Strengths:** The chapter follows the plan faithfully and with considerable skill. Rosa breaks into the flat, finds the letters, reads them, cries for the first time, is found by a builder, takes the letters, and has a partial breakthrough with her mother. The plan's key beats—the stained mattress, the letters to "C," the specific quotes from Izzy's letters, the builder finding her, the bus home—are all executed. The photograph revelation at the end (connecting back to Chapter 2's geocache) is a nice structural touch. The voice is largely consistent with Rosa's profile: the self-interrupting syntax, the compulsive repetition ("that I will never understand, never fully, never finally"), the physical specificity (wiping her nose on her sleeve, the cold stain through her jeans). The character work on Izzy through the letters is genuinely moving—the detail of watching Rosa at the crematorium from across the road, the "I can't stand being carried" line, the medical shorthand. These feel earned rather than manufactured. The scene with the builder is well-handled. "The surprise of finding something human where you expected emptiness" is a good line. His "You alright, love?" and Rosa's hatred of the diminutive while crying harder is psychologically precise. The mother scene at the end is restrained and effective—"My name. Again." is a good beat. **Weaknesses:** The chapter's most significant flaw is repetition that crosses from intentional stylistic choice into actual redundancy. The plan called for "repetition that is compulsive rather than rhythmic," but by the final third, the repetition becomes genuinely exhausting rather than emotionally resonant. "The too much, the too much, the too much" at the very end is the clearest example—it reads as a writer reaching for effect rather than achieving it. Similarly, "that I will never understand, never fully, never finally" and "that is here, that is here, that is here" are doing the same work in close proximity. The chapter also over-explains its own emotional logic. "This is new. This is the crying continuing, not the one-time thing I expected, not the breakthrough that leads to healing, to graduation, to better" is Rosa telling us what to think about her crying rather than letting the crying speak. The plan explicitly warned against "emotional graduation from bad to better" and "thematic overdetermination," but this passage commits both sins by naming them. Similarly, "the friendship that was real and insufficient, present and absent, everything and not-enough" is Rosa summarizing the theme rather than experiencing it. The dialogue is minimal but functional. "I'm a prospective tenant" lands well. The mother scene's dialogue is appropriately clumsy and real. The builder's "Fuck" is good. There's some purple prose in the final paragraphs—"the nothing, the everything, the too much" accumulates past the point of meaning. The plan called for "sentences fragment when she's avoiding feeling; run on when she's trying to outrun it," but the final paragraphs feel like they're running on past the point of control into actual incoherence. The chapter is not amateurish in its fundamentals—the craft is evident—but it does commit the amateur error of not trusting its own material. The letters are powerful enough; they don't need Rosa's extended commentary on what they mean. The crying scene is effective; it doesn't need to be explained as "not the breakthrough that leads to healing." The "forced poetry" problem is moderate. Most metaphors land ("the counting abandoned, the ceiling cracks uncounted"), but some don't ("the shoulds abandoned somewhere, the reservoir maybe, the hospital chapel"). The repetition of "nothing, everything, nothing" from Izzy's letter into Rosa's narration is clever the first time, overwrought by the fourth. Overall: a chapter that achieves its emotional goals more often than not, with a strong middle section and a weaker ending that over-explains and over-repeats. The character work is the strongest element; the structural self-consciousness is the weakest. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 14 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 8 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 9 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I don't go to the third location. This is not avoidance. This is the avoidance becoming something else, the search becoming not-search, the momentum that carried me to the reservoir, the salvage yard, the hospital chapel, the flat with the stained mattress, finally spent, finally still. Saturday. I work at the crematorium. I do my job correctly, which is worse than mistakes, the competence that feels like betrayal, like moving on, like being the person who continues while Izzy doesn't. Des is normal, which is a kindness I don't recognize until later, until I'm lying in bed with the letters on my chest, the weight of them, the lightness. After the funeral, the curtains closed, the conveyor belt humming, I find Des in the back, eating his sandwich, cheese no pickle, white bread slightly stale, the same sandwich every day, the routine that keeps him going, that keeps the crematorium going, that keeps everything going when everything should stop. "Your funeral," he says. Not a question. "Mr. Henderson. Lilies." "Alstroemeria," I say, the word his now, mine, something between us that isn't friendship, isn't anything I can name. He nods. Takes another bite. Chews longer than necessary, the silence not comfortable, not uncomfortable, just there, the silence of someone who has learned to be with things without needing to fill them. "My wife," he says. I freeze, the performance of not-listening ready, the deflection, the everything I do to not-hear. "Dead fifteen years. I talk to her in the car. She don't answer. I'm not sure I'd want her to, would I? Be weird, that." I look at him. Des, sixty, bad knees, large hands surprisingly gentle, the gentleness mechanical rather than tender. Not wise. Not a mentor. Just present, more present than I've been, than Izzy was, than anyone I know. "I found letters," I say. The words out before I can stop them, before I can perform not-telling, not-making-a-fuss. "From my friend. The one who—" I gesture, the reservoir, the drowning, the everything, the too much "—she wrote letters. To someone. Not me. She couldn't tell me." Des chews. Swallows. Looks at me with the look I've been getting, the seeing-through, but different, not seeing-through-me but seeing-me-through, the distinction I can't articulate, that I feel in my chest, my throat, my broken not-crying self. "People write things," he says. "Don't send them. Don't mean them. Mean them too much." He takes another bite. "My wife. I wrote her a letter once. Angry. Everything she did wrong. Never sent it. Burned it. Couldn't bear her knowing I thought it, couldn't bear not thinking it." I don't know what to say. The performance of response, the nod, the "yeah" at the right place, abandoned. I just stand there. The crematorium hums around us, the machinery, the ventilation, the everything that keeps going. "You're not putting that urn there," Des says, abrupt, normal, the moment passed. "Upper shelf. Mind your back." I move the urn. My back minds. I don't complain. This is the work, the physical, the present, the here. I go home. My mother has made dinner, slightly burned, pasta with sauce from a jar, her three meals in rotation, wrong, hers. We eat in silence that is not comfortable, that is never comfortable, that is something else now, something I can't name, the silence of two people who have things not-said, who may always have things not-said, who are trying, in their way, to be present. After, I take out my phone. The emojis still there. The letters on my bed upstairs, the photograph, the keys, the accumulation of objects that don't add up to understanding, that add up to something else, something I can't articulate, something about being present with not-knowing, with the ambiguity, with the everything is too much that doesn't resolve, doesn't graduate, doesn't become better. I don't go to the third location. I do. Both. I walk to the bus stop, the 52 not coming, I walk, through Hillsborough, through the city centre, past the places I know, the places I don't, the Sheffield that Izzy moved through without me, that I move through without her, the city continuing, indifferent, present. I find myself at the school. Our old school, the biology lab where we met, the dissected frog, the shared refusal to be squeamish, the beginning of something I thought was friendship, was best friendship, was everything, that I now can't name, can't categorize, can't resolve into meaning. I break in. The window round the back, the one we used for cigarettes, for sneaking out, for the small rebellions that felt large then, that feel nothing now, that feel everything. The lab is the same, the same tables, the same posters about cellular structure, the same smell of formaldehyde and adolescence, the same everything that is different because she is not here, because I am here without her, because the without is the point, the without is the grief, the without is everything. I look for the third object. The third emoji, the third clue, the third thing Izzy left for me to find. Nothing. No geocache box, no key, no notebook, no letter. I check under tables, behind posters, in the cupboard where they keep the frogs, the preserved things, the dead things that continue to be looked at, to be studied, to be present without being alive. Nothing. The nothing loud, present, everything. I kick a stool. It makes noise, falls, the clatter in the empty lab, the intrusion of sound where there should be nothing, where there is nothing, where I am nothing, everything, too much. I wait. Expect something to happen. Nothing happens. No security guard with tea, no builder letting me go, no object to find, no meaning to make. Just me, Rosa, seventeen, in a biology lab at night, with the emojis on my phone and the letters in my bag and the everything Izzy was and wasn't and wanted and didn't want and couldn't say and said too late and didn't say at all. I leave. On the way out, my hand in my bag, feeling for my keys, I touch something else. A phone. Not mine. Cheap, burner, the kind you buy with cash, the kind that exists in crime shows, in paranoia, in the hidden life Izzy lived that I didn't know, that I'm still discovering, still not understanding. I don't remember taking it. I don't remember seeing it. I hold it in the streetlight, the orange Sheffield light, and I know, I don't know, I can't know if I took it from the flat, from somewhere else, if it was in my bag already, if I'm losing something, if I've lost something, if the not-knowing is new or has always been there, the broken thing in me that doesn't know what it knows. One contact: Rosa. My name, just my name, no emoji, no nothing. One message draft, unsent: the three emojis, then words, deleted, the cursor blinking, then just the emojis again. Practice. She was practicing. She couldn't find the words. She found the symbol, the everything is too much, and she practiced sending it, and she didn't, or she did, or she scheduled it, or— I stare at it. The phone cheap in my hand, the screen small, the pixels visible, the emojis the same emojis, the same red drops, the same everything is too much that I still can't read, that I will never read, that I misread and misread and want to misread because the alternative, the alternatives, all of them, are worse. I walk home. The bus doesn't come. I don't expect it to. I walk through the city that continues, the streets that are the same streets, the grey that is the same grey, the March that won't become April properly. I have the burner phone in one hand, my own phone in the other, the two objects, the two Rosa's, the me I was and the me I am and the me Izzy saw and the me she didn't see and the me I'm becoming without knowing, without planning, without the performance of competence that Izzy maintained, that I tried to maintain, that fails me now, that I let fail. My mother is asleep, or pretending, the television off, the house quiet in a different way, not accusatory, not empty, just there, just present, just the place I live, the life I continue, the everything that doesn't stop even when it should. I lie on my bed. The burner phone on my chest, rising and falling with my breath, the cheap plastic warm from my hand. I don't look at it. I look at the ceiling. I don't count cracks. I don't count anything. The counting abandoned, the need to control, to organize, to make meaning where there isn't meaning, where there is something else, something I can't name, something about being present with the not-knowing, with the ambiguity, with the everything is too much that doesn't resolve, doesn't become less, doesn't become manageable, just is, just here, just now. I think about Izzy. The real Izzy, the one from the letters, the one who watched me at the crematorium, who couldn't come in, who wrote "I can't stand being carried" and meant it, who scheduled emojis and practiced messages and didn't send them, who drowned or didn't drown, who was sick and didn't tell me and I didn't ask and we failed together at friendship, at love, at the everything we were trying to be for each other, the everything that was real and insufficient and present and absent and everything and not-enough. I don't cry. This is not the not-crying of before. This is something else, the crying having happened, having passed, having become something I don't recognize, something that isn't absence, isn't presence, just is, just here, just the way I am now, the way I'll continue, the way the emojis continue, on my phone, on the burner, the everything is too much that I will carry, that I want to carry, that is my inheritance, my connection, my only geography in the world that has Izzy not in it, that has her everywhere, in objects and messages and scheduled emojis and brown water and the particular way the light hits the reservoir at dawn, the everything that doesn't resolve, that I don't want to resolve, that I want to misread forever because the misreading is the point, the misreading is the grief, the misreading is the story, the story that continues, that I continue, that we continue, the everything, the nothing, the too much, the too much, the too much.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter represents an ambitious attempt to execute a difficult tonal and structural brief: a chapter where "nothing happens" at the third location, where the protagonist finds a burner phone she doesn't remember taking, and where the emotional movement is internal rather than plot-driven. Let me assess it carefully. STRENGTHS: The Des scene is the chapter's strongest passage. The dialogue is genuinely good—"She don't answer. I'm not sure I'd want her to, would I? Be weird, that"—and Rosa's response, the words coming out before she can stop them, feels earned and true to character. The moment where Des pivots back to the urn ("You're not putting that urn there") is exactly the kind of deflection the plan called for, and it works. This is the chapter's most successful piece of showing-not-telling. The burner phone discovery is handled with appropriate ambiguity. Rosa not knowing if she took it, the deleted words, the practiced emojis—this is genuinely unsettling and serves the plan's requirement that the supernatural remain unconfirmed. The biology lab scene delivers on the plan's promise of "nothing"—no object, no meaning, just Rosa in an empty room. The kicked stool is a good physical beat. WEAKNESSES: The chapter suffers significantly from a stylistic problem that becomes increasingly severe as it progresses: the repetitive "the X, the Y, the Z" construction. This begins as a legitimate stylistic marker (the plan calls for run-ons when avoiding feeling) but by the final third it has become mechanical and self-parodying. Phrases like "the everything is too much" appear four or five times, "the misreading is the point, the misreading is the grief, the misreading is the story" is the kind of thematic overdetermination the plan explicitly warned against, and the final paragraph is a cascade of abstractions that collapses under its own weight. The plan specifically stated: "Things to avoid: Thematic overdetermination—some things just happen." The final paragraphs violate this repeatedly. "The misreading is the point, the misreading is the grief, the misreading is the story, the story that continues, that I continue, that we continue, the everything, the nothing, the too much, the too much, the too much" is exactly the kind of forced poeticism that the plan was designed to prevent. It tells us what to feel rather than creating the conditions for feeling. The opening section—"This is not avoidance. This is the avoidance becoming something else"—is clever but slightly too self-aware, too articulate for a character the plan describes as "not consistently articulate." The transition from "I don't go to the third location" to "I do. Both." is an interesting structural choice but feels slightly gimmicky rather than earned. The flashback fragment is absent. The plan called for a flashback in every chapter: "Izzy, always performing competence, always the one who knew what to say, what to do. Rosa less-together, grateful, sometimes resentful. A moment Rosa forgot: Izzy crying in the toilets at school, Rosa saying 'It'll be fine,' not asking what was wrong, wanting the performance to resume." This is entirely missing from the chapter, which is a significant deviation from the plan. The chapter also struggles with the "tell-don't-show" problem in its abstract passages. "The silence of two people who have things not-said, who may always have things not-said, who are trying, in their way, to be present" tells us about the dinner scene rather than rendering it. Compare this to the Des scene, which shows rather than tells. Rosa's voice becomes less distinctive as the chapter progresses—the specific, slightly-wrong observations that characterize her early in the plan give way to increasingly abstract formulations that sound more like literary prose than a seventeen-year-old's interior monologue. The chapter follows the plan's plot beats reasonably well (Des conversation, school break-in, nothing found, burner phone discovered) but the execution of the emotional arc is uneven, with the ending over-explaining what should be left implicit. OVERALL ASSESSMENT: This is a chapter with genuine strengths—the Des scene, the burner phone ambiguity, the physical specificity of the school break-in—undermined by a final third that abandons the plan's warnings about thematic overdetermination and purple prose. The missing flashback is a structural failure. The repetitive "the everything" construction starts as a voice marker and becomes a tic. The chapter earns some of its darkness but then over-explains it. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 12 Compelling Plot: 11 Coherent: 13 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 12 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 8 Purple Prose: 13 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 14 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 10 Characters Consistent with Profile: 13 Followed Chapter Plan: 10 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 14

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Saturday again. The week has passed without my noticing, the days bleeding into each other, the same days, the same grey, the same me continuing without knowing how. I work at the crematorium. I do my job correctly. This is not progress. This is not healing. This is just the work, the physical, the present, the here. Des is normal. He doesn't mention our conversation, the letters, the wife, the car. He arranges chairs, left not right, the curtains, the conveyor belt, the everything that continues. I put out hymn sheets. I say "sorry for your loss" to people who don't look at me. I mean it more than I used to, or less, or differently, I can't tell, the meaning of things shifting, the everything is too much becoming something I live with rather than something I solve. After work, I go home. My mother has made dinner, slightly burned, the pasta, the sauce from the jar, the same meal, the same wrong, the same hers. We eat in silence that is not comfortable, that is never comfortable, that is something we are trying, in our way, to make into something else, something that doesn't have a name, something that might be presence, might be love, might be just the continuation of two people who don't know how to be with each other but are trying, have always been trying, will continue trying without knowing if it works, if it matters, if anything matters. I take out my phone. The emojis still there. The burner phone in my drawer, the letters under my mattress, the photograph bent, the keys on my windowsill, the accumulation of objects that don't add up to understanding, that add up to something else, something about being here, being present, being Rosa without Izzy, being Rosa with Izzy everywhere, in the emojis, in the water, in the everything. I type a reply. Delete it. Type again. What I send: 🩸. Just one. Not three. One, because one is what I can manage, one is what I have, one is the everything is too much that I can still feel without being consumed by, or being consumed by anyway, but differently, the consumption becoming something else, something I live with, something that lives with me. Then: "You absolute dickhead." Then: "I found your letters. I hate you. I'm sorry. I don't know how to do this. The lilies were wrong again today." I don't expect an answer. I know there won't be an answer. Izzy is dead, is not dead, is somewhere I can't reach, that I couldn't reach when she was alive, that I failed to reach, that we failed together, the failure that is also love, also friendship, also everything we were and weren't to each other. The automated delivery report arrives. The buzz on my phone, the confirmation that the message reached somewhere, someone's system, the number still active, the infrastructure of communication continuing without the person, the ghost in the machine, the everything is too much that continues to be sent, to be received, to be present in the world that has Izzy not in it. I laugh. It becomes something else. I don't name it. Naming is what Izzy did, organizing, controlling, the performance of competence that hid the everything she couldn't manage, that I couldn't help her manage because I didn't know, because she didn't tell me, because I didn't ask, because we were both broken in our ways, performing fine, fine, everything fine, until we weren't, until she wasn't, until the water, the emojis, the scheduled message at 00:03 that I still can't read properly, that I will never read properly, that I want to misread forever. I schedule a message to myself. One year from now. I don't know what I write. I forget immediately, the content less important than the act, the reaching forward, the pretending I will be here in a year, that I will be different, that I will be the same, that I will be anything at all. The burner phone stays in my drawer. I check it sometimes. Nothing, then something, a wrong number, a spam text, I can't tell the difference, don't need to tell the difference, the ambiguity permanent, the not-knowing the point, the not-knowing the grief, the not-knowing the story. I go to the reservoir. Not to swim. Not to search. Just to stand at the edge in my work clothes, the black shoes I polish badly, the coat with the rip widening, the everything I am, present, here, now. The water is brown. Something floats near the far bank. I don't look at it properly. I've learned not to look at things floating in water, or I've unlearned, or I'm learning, the learning continuous, the everything is too much that doesn't resolve, doesn't graduate, doesn't become better, just becomes different, becomes this, becomes me standing at the edge of the water where my best friend died, not knowing if she meant to, not knowing if meaning matters, not knowing anything except the present, the cold, the water that doesn't know the difference, that doesn't know anything, that is not profound, that is just true. I don't go in. I don't leave. The bus doesn't come. This is normal, this is expected, this is the 52 that never comes when you need it, the Sheffield indifference, the everything that continues without you, that you continue within, the both, the neither, the too much. I walk home. The long way, through streets I don't know, through the city that continues, the grey that continues, the March that won't become April properly, that will become April eventually, that will become May, June, the year I scheduled the message for, the future I can't imagine, that I don't try to imagine, that I just move toward, one step, another step, the walking enough, the being enough, the enough that is not enough, that is everything, that is nothing, that is the too much I carry, that I want to carry, that is my inheritance, my connection, my only geography. My mother is awake when I get in. The kitchen, the mug, the television off. She looks at me. I look at her. The silence that is not comfortable, that is never comfortable, that is something we are making, have made, will continue making, the something that doesn't have a name, that might be love, might be habit, might be the only way we know to be with each other, the performance that becomes real through repetition, through continuation, through the not-giving-up that is also love, also everything. "You're back then." "Yeah." She gestures at the stove, at something, at nothing, at the everything she offers that I sometimes refuse, that I sometimes accept, that is always slightly wrong, slightly burned, slightly hers. "Food?" "Not hungry." "You're never hungry." The same statement, the same fact, but different now, or I hear it differently, or I want to hear it differently, the misreading that is the point, the misreading that is the grief, the misreading that is the story. I sit down anyway. She makes tea, her way, wrong, milk first, bag still in, no squeeze against the side. I don't correct her. This is her tea, her way, her presence, the only way she knows to be here, to be with me, to be in this world that has taken her husband, that has taken my friend, that continues to take, to give, to be indifferent, to be present, to be everything, nothing, too much. We drink in silence. The silence that is not comfortable, that is never comfortable, that is ours, that we are making into something, that might be enough, that might not be enough, that is enough for now, for this moment, for the present that is all we have, all I have, all I can manage, the everything is too much that I live with, that I want to live with, that is my connection to her, to Izzy, to my mother, to Des, to the bald girl in the hospital corridor, to the security guard with his flask, to the builder who let me go, to everyone who has seen me, who has seen through me, who has been present in their way, their insufficient, their everything way. I check my phone. The delivery report still there. The emojis still there. The message I sent, the one emoji, the dickhead, the everything I couldn't say, that I still can't say, that I said anyway, that is out there now, in the system, in the infrastructure, in the world that continues. I think about Izzy. The real Izzy, the one from the letters, the one who watched me at the crematorium, who couldn't come in, who wrote "I can't stand being carried" and meant it, who scheduled emojis and practiced messages and didn't send them, who drowned or didn't drown, who was sick and didn't tell me and I didn't ask and we failed together at friendship, at love, at the everything we were trying to be for each other. I think about her differently now. Not solved. Not understood. Present in her absence, absent in her presence, the ambiguity permanent, the not-knowing the point, the not-knowing the grief, the not-knowing the story that continues, that I continue, that we continue, the everything, the nothing, the too much. I don't count my breaths. I don't count ceiling cracks. I don't count anything. The counting abandoned, the need to control, to organize, to make meaning where there isn't meaning, where there is something else, something I can't name, something about being present with the not-knowing, with the ambiguity, with the everything is too much that doesn't resolve, doesn't become less, doesn't become manageable, just is, just here, just now. The tea goes cold.
