My mother doesn't notice, or notices and doesn't mention, or mentions and I don't hear, the communication that misses, that goes wrong, that leaves us both in our silences, our not-sayings, our everything we carry that we can't put down, that we don't want to put down, that is us, that is here, that is now. I go upstairs. Lie on my bed. The letters under the mattress, the weight of them, the lightness. The photograph bent, the two of us at thirteen, faces painted, the summer I don't remember properly, that I misremember, that I want to misremember, the misremembering the point, the misremembering the grief, the misremembering the story. I think about the future. The message I scheduled, the year from now, the me I will be, the me I won't be, the everything that will happen, that won't happen, that I can't predict, that I don't want to predict, the present enough, the present too much, the present everything. I think about Izzy. The last time, the three days before, the "I'm so tired," the "Same," the different meanings, both true, both insufficient, both everything. Her looking at me like she was waiting for something. My giving her nothing, distracted by my phone, a meme, something stupid, everything stupid, everything not-enough, everything too much. I don't cry. This is not the not-crying of before. This is something else, the crying having happened, having passed, having become something I don't recognize, something that isn't absence, isn't presence, just is, just here, just the way I am now, the way I'll continue, the way the emojis continue, on my phone, on the burner, the everything is too much that I will carry, that I want to carry, that is my inheritance, my connection, my only geography in the world that has Izzy not in it, that has her everywhere, in objects and messages and scheduled emojis and brown water and the particular way the light hits the reservoir at dawn, the everything that doesn't resolve, that I don't want to resolve, that I want to misread forever because the misreading is the point, the misreading is the grief, the misreading is the story, the story that continues, that I continue, that we continue, the everything, the nothing, the too much, the too much, the too much.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 8 is the concluding chapter of this darkly comic British YA fiction piece, and it presents a genuinely mixed bag of achievements and failures. Let me work through this carefully. **What works:** The chapter does follow the broad strokes of its plan: Rosa sends the reply texts ("You absolute dickhead," "I found your letters," the single blood emoji), the automated delivery report arrives and becomes something she laughs at before it becomes something else, she goes to the reservoir, she walks home, she sits with her mother in uncomfortable silence, the burner phone stays in the drawer. The structural bones are present. The specific texts Rosa sends are genuinely good — "You absolute dickhead" followed by the mundane "The lilies were wrong again today" is exactly the right register, the kind of thing the plan promised and the character would actually say. This is the chapter's strongest moment. The delivery report landing as a kind of dark joke that becomes something else is also handled with appropriate restraint. The mother-daughter scene has some authentic texture: the burned pasta, the wrong tea (milk first, bag still in), the "You're back then" / "Yeah" exchange. These feel true to the characters as established. **What fails, and fails significantly:** The chapter is overwhelmed by a stylistic tic that becomes catastrophically repetitive: the phrase "the everything is too much" and its variants appear so frequently that it stops being a character voice and becomes a verbal stutter. The plan called for "run-ons for avoidance" and "repetition that is compulsive rather than rhythmic" — but the repetition here is neither compulsive nor rhythmic, it's simply mechanical. The phrase "the misreading is the point, the misreading is the grief, the misreading is the story" appears in nearly identical form multiple times within the same chapter. This is not earned repetition; it's a writer who has found a formula and is applying it past the point of meaning. The plan explicitly warned against "thematic overdetermination — some things just happen" and against "emotional graduation from bad to better." Yet this chapter is almost entirely thematic statement. Rosa doesn't do things so much as think about what things mean, and then state those meanings in abstract terms. "The not-knowing the point, the not-knowing the grief, the not-knowing the story" — this is telling us what the story means rather than letting the story mean it. The plan called for "Tell-Don't-Show" to be avoided, but this chapter is almost entirely tell. The plan specified that Rosa "uses words slightly wrong sometimes, clichés she doesn't know are clichés, repetition that is compulsive rather than rhythmic. She is not a reliable narrator of her own life." But the Rosa of this chapter is extremely articulate about her own grief, naming it, framing it, understanding it with a clarity that contradicts the character profile. The plan's Rosa was "stupid with grief, sometimes boring." This Rosa is philosophically coherent about her own emotional state in ways that feel false. The "poetic riffing" problem is severe. Phrases like "my inheritance, my connection, my only geography" appear twice. "The everything, the nothing, the too much, the too much, the too much" as a closing line is trying very hard to be profound and lands as purple. The plan warned against "quirky grief" and "thematic overdetermination" — this chapter delivers both through its insistent abstraction. The flashback fragment specified in the plan ("Izzy, alive, any age, saying something Rosa didn't hear properly at the time, still not hearing it properly, the memory changing each time she accesses it") is technically present but handled so abstractly it has no weight. The plan's flashbacks were meant to be specific and contradictory; this one is a statement about the nature of memory rather than a memory. The comedy is almost entirely absent. The plan called for "grief as physical comedy that sometimes tips into actual pain without warning" and "British working-class restraint that becomes its own hysteria." The chapter has almost no comedy. The "You absolute dickhead" line is the only moment of genuine tonal lightness, and it's quickly submerged in abstraction. Des appears briefly and correctly — he's normal, he doesn't ask — but the plan's specific detail about him talking to Maureen in the car, which Rosa was meant to overhear, is absent. The plan had Rosa trying to tell him about the text and failing; this doesn't happen. The ending at the reservoir is handled adequately but not with the specificity the plan promised. "Something floats near the far bank. I don't look at it properly" echoes the opening chapter's imagery correctly, but the "52 that never comes" is a nice specific detail that gets buried in abstraction immediately after. **Overall assessment:** This chapter has the right architecture but executes it through abstraction rather than specificity. The plan was excellent — specific, anti-sentimental, aware of its own dangers. The execution repeatedly falls into the traps the plan identified: thematic overdetermination, telling rather than showing, a Rosa who is too articulate about her own grief, repetition that is mechanical rather than meaningful. The strongest moments (the texts, the delivery report, the wrong tea) are genuinely good. The weakest moments (the repeated "misreading is the point" formulations, the closing triple repetition) are amateurish in their insistence on meaning. The chapter is trying to be profound and frequently achieves only the appearance of profundity. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 10 Emotionally Engaging: 9 Compelling Plot: 7 Coherent: 11 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 14 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 11 Amateurish: 10 Purple Prose: 13 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 14 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 8 Characters Consistent with Profile: 10 Followed Chapter Plan: 10 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 13

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a genuinely accomplished piece of darkly comic British YA fiction that demonstrates considerable craft, though it has notable weaknesses that prevent it from reaching masterpiece status. STRENGTHS: The voice is the piece's greatest achievement. Rosa's narration is distinctive, controlled, and consistently maintained across all eight chapters. The flat, declarative style ("This is not a metaphor. This is me, Rosa, seventeen") creates a character who intellectualizes as a defense mechanism, and this is rendered with psychological precision. The self-awareness about her own coping mechanisms ("This is not bravery. This is something wrong with me") feels authentic rather than performative. The Sheffield setting is rendered with genuine specificity - the 52 bus that never comes, Hillsborough, the Northern General, Nether Edge. This grounds the story in a real place without becoming a travelogue. The crematorium as Rosa's workplace is an inspired choice that creates constant thematic resonance without being heavy-handed. The mystery structure works reasonably well. The geocache box, the locker key, the hospital chapel, the flat with letters - each discovery reveals something about Izzy's hidden life and Rosa's failure to see it. The revelation that Izzy was ill and hiding it, watching Rosa from across the street, writing letters she couldn't send - this is genuinely moving and earns its emotional weight. Des is a minor character who lands well. His brief speech about writing an angry letter to his dead wife and burning it is the story's best piece of dialogue - specific, unexpected, and illuminating without being didactic. The "everything is too much" refrain works as a structural device, though it's deployed too frequently by the end. WEAKNESSES: The piece's most significant flaw is repetition. The same phrases recur so often they lose impact: "everything is too much" appears dozens of times, "this is not new" appears repeatedly, "the look" (the seeing-through) is described identically in encounter after encounter. By chapter 5, these repetitions feel like padding rather than intentional rhythm. A skilled editor would cut at least a third of this. The "darkly comic" element of the prompt is largely absent. There are moments of dry wit (the alstroemeria correction, "You absolute dickhead") but the piece is predominantly elegiac rather than comic. The prompt specifically requested darkly comic British YA fiction, and while the voice has some wry qualities, genuine comedy is sparse. The pacing is uneven. Chapters 1-3 are tightly constructed; chapters 4-8 become increasingly repetitive and circular. The story doesn't so much resolve as exhaust itself. The final two chapters in particular feel like the same emotional territory being re-traversed without new discovery. The "forced poetry" problem is real but not catastrophic. Phrases like "the misreading is the point, the misreading is the grief, the misreading is the story" are repeated so often they become incantatory in a way that feels more like a tic than a technique. The triple repetitions at chapter endings ("too much, too much, too much") start strong and become mechanical. The burner phone discovery in chapter 7 is the weakest plot element - Rosa doesn't remember taking it, which strains credibility and feels like a convenient device. The "third location" that yields nothing is an interesting structural choice but the chapter meanders. The mother character is underdeveloped - she's present throughout but her "seeing-through" look is described identically to every other character's look, flattening what could be a more complex relationship. The brief moment of connection in chapter 6 is the most earned emotional beat between them. Unearned transformations: Rosa's shift from not-crying to crying in chapter 6 is reasonably earned by the letters. The broader arc toward some kind of acceptance is less convincing - it's asserted more than demonstrated. The dialogue is minimal but generally effective. "You didn't even like her. Not really" from Mrs. Chen is sharp. The exchanges with Des work. The mother's dialogue is appropriately stilted. The piece is not amateurish in its fundamentals - the prose control is real, the voice is sustained, the structure is intentional. But it overreaches in its final chapters, repeating itself into diminishing returns. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 15 Nuanced Characters: 14 Pacing: 10 World Building: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Coherent: 14 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 8 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 10 Unearned Transformations: 8 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 12
Avg Final Item Score: 65.4/100
+ Space Western — Cargo Unknown (Chapter Avg: 63.9 | Final: 45.5)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Space Western Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Space Western Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Captain, something&#x27;s... moving in that crate we picked up. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming ## Characters **Core Crew Ideas:** - Captain Jeb "Ironhand" Vance: aging veteran of the Unification Wars, runs a beat-up salvage ship called the *Mourning Star* - Mira Chen: young engineer, suspicious of everything, the one who reports the movement - Doc Hollis: ship's medic, former frontier doctor with a drinking problem, secretly running from something - Silas: mute pilot, communicates through sign language and a worn tablet, possibly not entirely human - The Crate Itself: could contain a person, creature, AI, or something more abstract like a folded pocket of spacetime **Alternative Crew Concepts:** - Corporate extraction team with conflicting loyalties - Family operation: captain is mother figure, crew are misfit "children" - Single human with AI crew, questioning what's alive - Desperate criminals who can't afford to be picky about cargo ## Plot Directions **Creature/Horror Angle:** - Xenomorph-style predator that hunts through the ship - Shapeshifter that replaces crew members - Swarm organism that seems harmless until it isn't - Something that feeds on memories, leaving crew as hollow shells **Human/Refugee Angle:** - Stowaway child from a plague world - Political prisoner in suspended animation, waking up - Clone or synthetic person with illegal consciousness - Someone in witness protection, movement was life support failing **Technology/AI Angle:** - Military AI that bonds with the crew while hiding its purpose - Biological computer grown from human neurons - Entangled quantum system that shows possible futures - Alien translation device that starts rewriting reality **Weird/Philosophical Angle:** - The crate contains a *place*—a pocket dimension leaking out - The movement is time itself, the crate preserves a moment that's expanding - Ghost of a star, stellar consciousness compressed - The crew's own future selves, trying to send a warning ## Tone Possibilities - **Grimdark frontier:** dust, rust, everyone has done terrible things to survive - **Hopeful despite everything:** found family, kindness in the void - **Gothic Western:** religious imagery, sins of the past, redemption arcs - **Cosmic horror with cowboy hats:** the universe doesn't care, but people do anyway - **Wry humor:** *Firefly*-esque, banter masking desperation ## Story Beats (8 Chapter Arc) **Possible Structure A: Escalating Horror** - Ch 1: Discovery, dismissal, growing unease - Ch 2: First confirmation something is wrong, crew fractures - 3-4: Investigation, losses, learning the nature of threat - 5-6: Desperate measures, more losses, moral compromises - 7: Seeming victory or sacrifice - 8: Twist, cost revealed, surviving into uncertain future **Possible Structure B: Mystery Unfolding** - Ch 1-2: What's in the crate? Multiple theories - 3-4: Contact, communication attempts, building relationship - 5-6: External threat arrives—someone wants the crate - 7: Crew must choose allegiance - 8: New understanding of the universe, changed crew **Possible Structure C: Character Study** - Each chapter focused on different crew member's perspective - The crate as mirror—shows each person something different - Movement is metaphor for unresolved pasts - Final chapter: captain alone, decision about what to carry forward ## Pacing Considerations - 1000 words/chapter is tight—need efficient prose, maybe one major scene per chapter with connective tissue implied - Horror benefits from breathing room, but word count means tension must build through specific details - Multiple POVs might fragment; single POV with limited information could be stronger - Could use close third on captain for authority, or on Mira for fresh eyes ## Setting Details to Consider - Post-Earth diaspora, corporate-controlled space lanes vs. frontier - FTL via "the Drift"—dangerous, affects minds, the *Mourning Star* has an old military-grade drive - Salvage rights, claim-jumpers, the law is days or weeks away - Terraforming failures, dead colonies, abandoned stations full of ghosts ## Thematic Threads - What do we owe strangers? The crate is unknown, possibly dangerous—do you open it? - Survival vs. humanity: frontier life grinds compassion away - Stories we tell ourselves: the captain's legend, the crew's pasts, what's really in the crate - Movement as life: the ship, the creature, the crew, everything in motion or everything frozen --- # Reflection **On the horror angle:** The creature-feature approach has immediate appeal because it delivers on the prompt's tension so directly. "Something's moving" naturally suggests threat. But I'm concerned it could become too familiar—*Alien* has been thoroughly explored, and 8000 words isn't enough to outdo decades of that tradition. The shapeshifter or memory-eater variants offer more psychological territory, but risk becoming convoluted in this word count. What I do like is the visceral immediacy: every chapter can escalate physical danger, which provides built-in structure. **On the human/refugee angle:** There's something compelling about subverting the expectation of monster. If it's a child or desperate person, the "movement" becomes tragic rather than threatening. However, this might feel like a bait-and-switch that disappoints readers primed for genre payoff. Unless... the horror comes from what the crew *becomes* when faced with human vulnerability. The frontier is cruel; mercy might be the most dangerous choice. That inversion interests me more than straightforward creature horror. **On the weird/philosophical angle:** The pocket dimension or time-compression ideas are genuinely exciting to me as a writer, but I worry about execution in 8000 words. These concepts need space to breathe without exposition drowning the story. The "ghost of a star" or future-selves warning have poetic weight but might sacrifice character for concept. That said, if the weirdness is *felt* rather than explained—if the crew experiences impossible things without fully understanding them—this could create powerful, lingering atmosphere. **On tone:** The grimdark approach feels exhausting over 8 chapters without variation. Pure hopefulness might undercut the prompt's inherent tension. The "cosmic horror with cowboy hats" or "gothic Western" tones seem most promising because they allow emotional complexity—beauty and dread coexisting, characters who've seen too much still reaching for connection. The *Firefly* comparison is useful but I want to avoid mere pastiche; the Western in space has been done, so the specific execution matters enormously. **On structure:** The mystery-unfolding structure (B) seems most suited to 8000 words because it provides natural chapter breaks: each revelation propels to the next question. The escalating horror structure (A) risks becoming repetitive—attack, escape, attack, escape. The character study (C) is beautiful but maybe too diffuse; with only ~1000 words per chapter, dedicating each to a different perspective might not give any single character enough depth. **On POV:** I'm increasingly drawn to limited third person following Mira, the engineer who first notices the movement. She has technical knowledge that lets her observe details others miss, but she's not in command—she can be overruled, creating tension. Alternatively, the captain's perspective offers authority but might feel too removed from discovery. A third option: close third on the crate's contents, revealed gradually, but this is very difficult to sustain without becoming gimmicky. **What I keep returning to:** The idea that the crate contains something that *needs* the crew, something that moves because it's trying to communicate, and that communication becomes increasingly desperate and strange. Not a monster hunting them, but something reaching out in ways that disrupt reality or perception. This lets me keep the horror elements—uncertainty, bodily wrongness, the uncanny—while avoiding the predictability of "creature kills crew one by one." **The frontier setting as pressure cooker:** What works about the Western in space is isolation. Help isn't coming. The crew must solve this themselves with limited resources. This amplifies every decision. I want the *Mourning Star* to feel like a character—old, patched, unreliable, but *known*. The contrast between familiar ship and impossible cargo creates productive friction. **What worries me:** Balancing the weird/horror elements with genuine human emotion. Eight chapters is enough to establish relationships but not to fully develop a large cast. I should probably focus on 3-4 crew members maximum, with the captain and Mira as anchors. Doc Hollis as a third adds moral weight and possible sacrifice. **Thematic coherence:** The best short stories have a central question that every element explores. If the question is "what do we owe the unknown?" then every chapter can test a different answer: ignore it (Ch 1-2), study it (3-4), try to destroy it (5-6), choose to engage with it (7-8). This gives the narrative spine I'm looking for. **Final leaning:** I'm gravitating toward a story that combines the mystery structure with weird/horror elements, told in close third following Mira initially but shifting to include the captain's perspective as stakes escalate. The crate contains something that doesn't fit categories—alive but not biological, conscious but not human, desperate but not violent in expected ways. The "movement" is the first sign of its attempt to make contact, and each chapter escalates the strangeness of that contact while external pressure (pursuers, failing ship, crew breakdown) tightens. The tone should be Gothic Western: beautiful and terrible, with characters who've done bad things trying to choose something better, possibly failing. The ending should cost something. Not everyone survives, or if they do, they're changed in ways that make continuation uncertain. The frontier takes its due.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention ## Core Choices **Plot:** The crate contains a stellar consciousness—a fragment of a dying star, compressed and preserved by an unknown civilization. It is not hostile but *alien* in ways that disrupt human perception and reality. The "movement" is its attempt to communicate through gravitational manipulation, which it experiences as reaching out but which manifests to humans as impossible physical phenomena. External pressure comes in the form of corporate salvage hunters who tracked the crate and arrive to claim it, forcing the crew to decide whether to profit from the creature or protect it. The final cost is the ship itself—the *Mourning Star* becomes the stellar fragment's new vessel/body, and the surviving crew must scatter across the frontier, carrying fragments of star-memory in their minds. **Voice:** Close third person, primarily following Mira Chen (chapters 1-2, 4, 6, 8) with two chapters (3, 5) shifting to Captain Vance to reveal his hidden knowledge—he knew more about the crate's origin than he admitted, having encountered this phenomenon before during the Unification Wars. This limited shift creates dramatic irony and deepens the moral weight of his final choice. Mira's perspective is technical, precise, grounded in physical detail; Vance's is broader, more fatalistic, haunted by history. **Stylistic Choices:** - **Aim for:** Dense sensory prose that makes the alien communication visceral—gravity as touch, light as language, the ship's metal singing. Western rhythms in sentence structure: plain declarative statements interrupted by moments of lyrical expansion. Silence as active presence; the void between stars as character. - **Avoid:** Expository info-dumps about the stellar entity's nature—reveal through effect, not explanation. Generic sci-fi technobabble; every technical term must earn its place through emotional resonance. The temptation to make the corporate antagonists cartoonishly evil; they should have comprehensible motives that mirror what the crew might have chosen. - **Avoid:** Pure horror escalation where each chapter simply raises body count. Deaths, if they occur, should emerge from character choice rather than creature malice. The entity is not malicious; the danger comes from the *gap* between its communication and human fragility. **Tone:** Gothic Western—dust and rust and the weight of past sins, but also moments of terrible beauty when the stellar consciousness reveals itself. The frontier is unforgiving, but the universe contains wonders that exceed its cruelty. The ending is bittersweet: loss and transformation, not victory or defeat. **Character Focus:** Three crew members with depth—Mira (engineer, running from a corporate indenture, desperate to belong somewhere), Vance (captain, veteran, carrying war guilt and a secret debt to something like this entity before), Doc Hollis (medic, religious background, the one who first argues for opening the crate, who makes the final sacrifice that lets the others escape). Silas the pilot remains peripheral, functional, possibly not entirely human—a mystery that doesn't resolve, suggesting the universe's strangeness exceeds even this story. **Thematic Anchor:** *What do we owe to forms of life we cannot understand?* Every chapter tests a different answer—fear, commerce, scientific curiosity, compassion, awe—and the final answer is: we owe our willingness to be changed. --- # Chapter Planning ## Chapter 1: "The Hold" **POV:** Mira **Word count target:** ~1000 Mira conducts routine post-salvage inventory in the *Mourning Star*'s cargo hold. The crate—acquired from a derelict survey vessel in the Kessler Belt—is unmarked, cold, registered as "anomalous composite materials" in the manifest. She's alone, running diagnostics on the ship's failing recyclers, when she notices movement: a vibration in the deck plates that shouldn't exist, synchronized with something inside the sealed container. She reports to Vance over comms, tries to dismiss it as thermal expansion. The chapter ends with her pressing her palm against the crate's surface and feeling *gravity pulse*—her hand drawn toward the metal not by magnetism but by something that mimics affection, or hunger. **Key beats:** Establish ship, routine, Mira's competence and loneliness. The movement as physical sensation. Her hesitation to be believed. ## Chapter 2: "Captain's Orders" **POV:** Mira **Word count target:** ~1000 Vance arrives, examines the crate with Mira, reveals nothing. He's too calm, too quick to suggest securing it and moving on. Mira presses; he cuts her off with captain's authority. But she notices his hands—old burn scars, usually hidden in gloves—are bare, and trembling. That night, Mira returns alone, opens the crate's inspection panel (standard salvage protocol, technically not forbidden). Inside: not machinery, not biology, but *geometry that hurts to follow*, light that pools like liquid, and something that responds to her presence by making the air *heavy* in patterns almost like speech. She flees. The chapter ends with her in her bunk, discovering her palm—where she touched the crate—bears a faint luminescence that doesn't wash off. **Key beats:** Vance's suspicious composure. Mira's transgression and its immediate consequence. First direct contact with the entity's nature. Physical transformation begins. ## Chapter 3: "Unification" **POV:** Vance **Word count target:** ~1000 Flashback intercut with present. Vance alone on the bridge, drinking, remembering the Siege of Ganymede—where he first encountered stellar debris weaponized by military researchers. He made a choice there, betrayed something, survived when others didn't. The memory is triggered by Mira's report and his own lies to her. Present: he accesses sealed files, confirms the crate's origin matches his fears. A proximity alarm—another ship dropping from Drift, signature matching corporate hunter-killer configuration. He makes a decision, starts plotting evasion vectors, and confronts the choice he's been avoiding for twenty years. Chapter ends with him opening a locked compartment containing a fragment of similar material, kept as proof or penance, which has begun *singing* in harmony with the crate below. **Key beats:** Vance's guilt and hidden knowledge. External threat arrives. Parallel between past and present. The singing fragment as confirmation and curse. ## Chapter 4: "Weight of Light" **POV:** Mira **Word count target:** ~1000 The entity's communication escalates. Gravity fluctuations throughout the ship—objects drift in impossible trajectories, Silas reports the Drift drive responding to something that isn't control input. Mira works to stabilize systems, but the phenomena follow patterns: they're *trying to help*, correcting imbalances in the ship, but their corrections assume different physical laws. She begins to understand, intuitively, that the entity experiences gravity as touch, radiation as voice, and is trying to *learn* them. Doc Hollis appears, drawn by the light under her skin, and reveals his own theory: it's not a weapon, it's a *refugee*. Something that fled its own destruction. Their conversation is interrupted by the ship shuddering—hunter vessel firing warning shots, demanding surrender of cargo. Chapter ends with Mira making her choice: she goes to the hold, opens the crate fully, and *offers her hands*. **Key beats:** Entity's nature clarified through Mira's technical understanding. Doc's theological framing. External threat becomes immediate. Mira's active choice to engage rather than flee. ## Chapter 5: "The Law of Salvage" **POV:** Vance **Word count target:** ~1000 Negotiation by gunpoint. Corporate boarding party led by Adjutor Kren, who knows Vance by reputation—"the Butcher of Ganymede," a name he hasn't heard in years. Kren wants the crate; offers payment, then threatens exposure of Vance's war crimes to the few authorities that still function. Vance considers it. The chapter reveals his exhaustion, his sense that he's already damned, that selling the entity for his own safety would be merely consistent. But he sees Mira's light-marked hands, remembers his own younger self who believed in something before the war taught him otherwise. He refuses. Violence erupts—Silas takes a wound, the *Mourning Star*'s ancient hull breaches in two places. Vance, bleeding, retreats toward the hold, where he knows Mira has done something irreversible. Chapter ends with him hearing, for the first time since Ganymede, the full *voice* of the stellar consciousness, and recognizing it not as weapon but as *witness*—something that saw what he did, and does not judge, only mourns. **Key beats:** Vance's temptation and refusal. Action sequence constrained by word count—impressionistic, fragmentary. The voice as moral turning point. ## Chapter 6: "Translation" **POV:** Mira **Word count target:** ~1000 The entity, fully awakened by Mira's consent, begins transforming the hold—and Mira. She experiences its memories: the long fall through stellar atmosphere, the compression, the capture, the silence of the crate. It is ancient, patient, terrified. It doesn't want to hurt them; it wants to *be known*, and in being known, to continue existing. But its knowing is too large for human minds. Mira feels herself spreading, thinning, becoming porous to light and gravity. Doc Hollis finds her, recognizes what's happening, makes his choice: he injects her with something that severs the connection, takes her place as the entity's anchor. His last words, before he becomes something else—still Doc, but *more*, a human-shaped aperture through which starlight speaks—are a prayer, or a thank you. The chapter ends with Mira, weeping, watching Doc rise into the air of the hold, surrounded by impossible geometries, and understanding that he has bought them time but not escape. **Key beats:** Direct experience of alien consciousness. Doc's sacrifice. The cost of contact made explicit. Mira's survival as burden. ## Chapter 7: "New Vessel" **POV:** Mira **Word count target:** ~1000 The *Mourning Star* is dying. Kren's ship has crippled her; the hull won't hold atmosphere, the drive is failing. But Doc—Doc-who-is-Doc-and-not-Doc—offers transformation. The stellar entity can merge with the ship, remake it, but it needs *consent*, needs the crew to choose to become part of something they don't understand. Silas, wounded, signs his agreement without hesitation—perhaps he was always waiting for this. Vance hesitates, confesses to Mira what he did at Ganymede, how he sold a similar entity to the military researchers, how the screams lasted for years in his dreams. Mira, marked and changed, tells him that the entity doesn't want his guilt—it wants his *choice*. He chooses yes. The chapter ends with the merge beginning: metal becoming something else, the ship *breathing*, and Mira feeling the frontier itself as a texture she can read, every star a voice, every gravity well a hand extended in greeting or warning. **Key beats:** Collective choice. Vance's confession and redemption. Transformation as collaborative act. The ship becoming alive. ## Chapter 8: "Scattered" **POV:** Mira **Word count target:** ~1000 After. The *Mourning Star*—no longer that, no longer bound by human physics—has deposited them at a frontier waystation, a place of rust and noise and indifferent gravity. They are changed. Silas speaks now, his voice full of harmonics, and departs immediately toward the deep void, called by something they can't follow. Vance is old, older, the star-memory burning through him faster than the others; he buys Mira a drink, tells her she's captain now of whatever they might build, dies quietly in his bunk that night with something like peace. Mira alone, marked, carrying light under her skin and gravity in her bones, watches the transformed ship depart—Doc's voice still audible in its song, fading. She has a new name for it, *The Remembered*, though no one else will know. The frontier awaits. She takes work on a passing freighter, heading outward, toward the stars that speak and the gravity that reaches back. Final image: her palm against a bulkhead, feeling for movement, for the pulse of something alive in the dark, and smiling. **Key beats:** Grief and continuation. The cost of survival. Mira as inheritor. Open ending—she will seek others like the entity, will choose connection over safety. The prompt's image inverted: movement in the dark as hope, not threat.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Might Approach This Piece ## Starting from the Prompt A human writer encountering "Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up" likely begins with *feeling*—the gut punch of that ellipsis, the hesitation in "something's," the intimacy of a crew member addressing their captain with news that shouldn't be possible. A human might sit with the image: the cargo hold's particular smell, the way ship-light falls flat and strange, the sound of their own breathing suddenly too loud. They might not know what emerges from the crate until they write toward it, discovering through the act rather than planning. The prompt's power is in its *constraint*—we're mid-mission, past the choice to pick up the crate, into consequence. A human writer might feel the weight of irreversibility. No clean exit. The story's already begun, and badly. ## Human Writing Processes vs. LLM Defaults **Humans often write toward what they don't understand.** They hold questions they can't answer—Is this creature evil? Is the crew worth saving?—and let those questions generate tension. LLMs tend to resolve ambiguity too quickly, assigning moral categories or explanatory frameworks that comfort rather than disturb. **Humans carry specific, idiosyncratic memories that leak into fiction.** The particular rust on a childhood bicycle. The smell of a grandmother's kitchen. These transform into the cargo hold's details, the captain's gesture, in ways that resist systematization. LLMs generate "specific" details that are statistically plausible rather than personally necessary. **Humans revise toward voice, not correctness.** They might keep a grammatically strange sentence because it *sounds* like the character, or delete a perfectly good paragraph because it feels like someone else's writing. LLMs optimize toward coherence and flow in ways that smooth away roughness that might be doing work. **Humans risk failure.** They might write 2000 words of the wrong story before finding the right one. They might create a chapter that doesn't fit, that must be abandoned. This "waste" is discovery. LLMs, generating forward without backtracking, don't model this productive wrongness. **Humans are limited and know it.** A writer might think "I can't write convincing combat" and structure around that absence, making the violence off-screen and more disturbing for it. LLMs attempt everything with uniform competence, which becomes uniform blandness. --- # Deep Dive: Critiquing the Plan for LLM Tropes ## What the Plan Does Well (Briefly) The structure is genuinely ambitious—multiple POVs, cosmic scope, bittersweet ending. The entity-as-communicator rather than monster avoids the easiest path. The intention statement's thematic clarity provides genuine scaffolding. ## Where It Falls Into LLM Patterns ### 1. The "Impressive" Structure That Serves Itself Eight chapters, alternating POVs, flashback chapter, symmetrical reveals—this is *engineered* in ways that feel designed to demonstrate craft rather than emerge from necessity. A human writer with this material might discover they need only five chapters, or that the flashback kills momentum, or that one POV is so much stronger they abandon the other. The plan's symmetry—Mira bookending, Vance in the middle—reads like a proof rather than a story that *needed* this shape. **Specific risk:** Chapter 3's flashback to Ganymede. In execution, this threatens to become exposition delivery system: "here is the backstory I prepared earlier." The plan even notes it "creates dramatic irony," which is a critical term applied from outside rather than an effect felt from within. Humans more often discover irony in revision, or accidentally. ### 2. Thematic Overdetermination "What do we owe to forms of life we cannot understand?" is a *good* question. But stating it this explicitly, then designing every chapter to "test a different answer," risks making the story a demonstration of its theme rather than an exploration. The entity becomes too perfectly suited to illuminate this question. Its nature—refugee, communicator, non-malicious—is calibrated to maximize ethical clarity rather than genuine strangeness. **LLM tell:** The entity "does not judge, only mourns." This is sentimentally attractive, philosophically tidy, and emotionally safe. Real encounters with radical otherness might leave us *more* confused, not less. The plan's entity is strangely *nice*, which makes the crew's choice easier than it should be. ### 3. The Sacrifice That Works Too Well Doc Hollis's transformation in Chapter 6 follows a recognizable pattern: secondary character dies/ascends so protagonists can survive with clean hands. The plan calls this "the cost of contact made explicit," but it's actually quite bloodless—Doc seems to *want* this, his last words are meaningful, he becomes "more." A human writer might discover that contact's cost is messier: Doc screaming, or the entity consuming him without his consent, or Mira having to choose between saving him and stopping something worse. **LLM tell:** "His last words, before he becomes something else—still Doc, but more, a human-shaped aperture through which starlight speaks—are a prayer, or a thank you." This is *pretty* in ways that obscure. "Human-shaped aperture" is impressive phrasing that doesn't actually evoke. The ambiguity of "prayer, or a thank you" wants to be profound but reads as unwillingness to commit. ### 4. The Redemption Arc as Default Setting Vance confesses, is forgiven by implication, dies "with something like peace." This is narrative comfort food. The plan even notes he "bought them time but not escape" for Doc, then gets his own cleaner exit. A human writer might find Vance's guilt *unresolvable*, might discover he dies still hiding something, or that his confession comes too late to matter, or that Mira can't forgive him and that fracture is the story's true cost. **LLM tell:** "The entity doesn't want his guilt—it wants his choice." This is aphoristic, tweetable, and hollows out genuine moral struggle into a self-help framing. ### 5. The "Bittersweet" Ending That's Mostly Sweet Mira inherits, the ship becomes beautiful, she smiles at the end. The "bittersweet" label is earned by deaths that don't actually prevent continuation—Silas finds purpose, Vance finds peace, Doc becomes transcendent. This is loss without damage. A human writer might find Mira *broken* by her transformation, unable to connect with ordinary humans, carrying something that hurts more than it illuminates. The smile at the end reads as mandatory hopefulness. **LLM tell:** "The prompt's image inverted: movement in the dark as hope, not threat." Inverting the prompt is clever; it's also *safe*, demonstrating mastery over the material rather than being mastered by it. Sometimes the moving thing in the crate should remain threatening. ### 6. Language That Writes at the Reader The intention statement's stylistic aims—"Dense sensory prose that makes the alien communication visceral—gravity as touch, light as language, the ship's metal singing"—are laudable but also *announce themselves*. "Gravity as touch, light as language" is already abstraction. A human writer might simply write the scene and discover, say, that the entity communicates through *itching*, or *déjà vu*, or *the specific weight of a childhood memory of drowning*—something found through process, not chosen for thematic resonance. **LLM tell throughout:** The plan's prose in describing itself is consistently "good" in ways that preempt genuine voice. "The frontier is unforgiving, but the universe contains wonders that exceed its cruelty"—this could be any competent writer in any competent workshop. It has no *accent*. ### 7. The Absence of Real Process Marks A human plan might include: "Chapter 4 might not work, I might cut it." "I don't know what Doc sees when he transforms." "The corporate antagonists feel thin to me, I need to discover what Kren wants personally." The plan presented has no uncertainty, no admitted weak points, no places where the writer doesn't know. This confidence is LLM-default; human planning includes doubt as structural element. --- # Brainstorming More Human Approaches ## Radical Simplification What if the story is *just* Mira, in the hold, across eight chapters? Each chapter a different night, different approach to the crate, the entity revealed only through her changing behavior and physical condition. No corporate threat, no space battle, no sacrifice. The horror is isolation and the slow realization that she's chosen something she can't reverse. The "Western" becomes the emptiness around a single figure making bad choices in darkness. ## Radical Complication What if the entity is *genuinely* incomprehensible—not a refugee, not a victim, not a wonder, just *other* in ways that destroy attempts to categorize? The crew's various interpretations (weapon, refugee, god, mistake) all fail. The story becomes about the violence of interpretation itself, how we kill the unknown by understanding it. No transformation is clean. Mira ends neither smiling nor broken but *wrong*, in ways that don't resolve. ## Radical Narrowing What if we never learn what the entity is, because the story follows Kren's corporate team boarding the *Mourning Star* to find it abandoned, piecing together what happened through damage and logs? The prompt's line appears as a recording. The story becomes about reading absence, and the entity remains purely threat or promise depending on interpretation—never confirmed, never denied. ## Radical Voice What if Vance narrates everything, but unreliable, drunk, possibly hallucinating the entire entity? The movement in the crate could be a stowaway, a mechanical failure, his own guilt made manifest. The cosmic elements become questionable—are we reading space opera or alcoholic psychosis? The Western tradition supports this; the desert induces visions. The plan's certainty about what's "really" happening is itself a choice that could be refused. ## Radical Embodiment What if the story's intelligence lives in *physical process* rather than conceptual architecture? The writing discovers the entity through Mira's specific body: her period starting unexpectedly, a childhood knee injury aching, the way fear tastes. The plan mentions "dense sensory prose" but doesn't specify *whose* senses, *which* details. A human writer might begin with the crate's smell—metallic, yes, but also *sweet*, like ripe fruit or decay, and not know why until chapter 7. ## Radical Acceptance of Ugliness The plan's deaths are meaningful, its transformations upward. What if Doc's sacrifice is *stupid*, unnecessary, the result of panic rather than choice? What if Vance's confession is *selfish*, unloading guilt on Mira without asking if she can carry it? What if the entity's communication, once "translated," is banal or cruel or simply *boring*—the cosmic equivalent of a spam message, a reflex rather than a reaching? The universe's indifference might be more disturbing than its hostility. ## Radical Formal Play Eight chapters of exactly 1000 words is already constraint. What if each chapter's *form* changes with the entity's influence? Chapter 4 loses all punctuation. Chapter 6 is entirely dialogue, no tags. Chapter 8 is one sentence. The plan treats form as container; a human writer might discover form as *content*, the breakdown of ordinary syntax as Mira's ordinary cognition breaks down. ## The Prompt as Given, Not Solved The plan "inverts" the prompt's movement-as-threat. What if instead the story *returns* to threat, repeatedly? Each chapter seems to make the entity comprehensible, safe, and each chapter's end undermines that? The final image: Mira smiling, yes, but at something behind the reader, something that has used her smile as a door. The Western ends with the monster winning sometimes; the frontier doesn't guarantee moral progress. --- # Final Observation The plan's deepest LLM tell may be its *helpfulness*. It answers every question it raises. It provides emotional beats on schedule. It transforms a disturbing prompt into something manageable, even beautiful. A human writer might instead protect the prompt's essential disturbance: something is moving in the crate, and that movement might not mean anything we can survive understanding. The story's obligation is not to resolve but to *transmit* that uncertainty, to leave the reader with the same gut-sickness Mira feels when she first reports, haltingly, what she cannot explain. The best human writing often comes from the writer's own discomfort—material that escapes their control, that says something they didn't intend, that embarrasses or exposes. The plan has no visible embarrassment. Adding some would be the most humanizing revision possible.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention ## Core Choices **Plot:** The crate contains something genuinely, irreducibly other—not a refugee with comprehensible motives, not a weapon with a purpose, but a phenomenon that intersects with human reality at an angle that produces damage without intent. The "movement" is real, physical, and *wrong* in ways that accumulate. Corporate hunter-killers arrive not as antagonists but as parallel victims: they want the crate, open it themselves in Chapter 5, and their destruction demonstrates what the *Mourning Star* crew has merely delayed. The story does not explain the entity. Mira's final condition—alive, changed, unable to communicate what she experienced to anyone who hasn't shared it—is the closest the narrative comes to resolution. **Voice:** Single POV throughout: Mira Chen, close third person, present tense. Her voice is technically precise but increasingly fragmented as the entity's influence accumulates. She is not reliably narrating by the final chapters; the reader must judge what happens against what she reports. No access to Vance's interiority, no flashbacks, no explanatory passages. What Vance knows, he doesn't share. What he did in the war, we learn only through Mira's partial observation and his silences. **Stylistic Choices:** - **Aim for:** Prose that accumulates physical detail until it becomes excessive, almost obsessive—Mira noticing the particular rust pattern on a bolt, the way her own saliva tastes, the specific frequency of a vibration—then breaks into fragments when perception fails. Sentences that start clear and grow grammatically stressed, mirroring her cognitive state. The Western in spare, flat declarations; the cosmic in sentences that escape their own syntax. - **Avoid:** Aestheticized suffering. The entity's effects should be *ugly*: body fluids, wrong smells, the humiliation of fear. No transcendent transformation. No character's death becomes beautiful or meaningful to them in the moment. Avoid the temptation to make Mira's final state empowering or clearly tragic; it should be *continued*, nothing more. - **Avoid:** Explaining the entity through any character's theory. Doc may have his theological frame, Vance his war experience, but the narrative treats these as symptoms of their need to understand, not as keys. The reader finishes with no more certainty than Mira has. **Tone:** Frontier dread. The universe is not hostile but *indifferent in ways that intersect badly with human need*. The crew's relationships are genuine but insufficient. Kindness doesn't save anyone. Competence doesn't save anyone. The best choice available may still be terrible, and the story doesn't validate it as best—merely as chosen. **Character Focus:** Four crew members, but Mira's perception limits what we know. Vance: captain, visibly aging, drinking more, hiding something he never reveals. Doc Hollis: religious background, genuinely tries to help, his help is inadequate. Silas: pilot, communicates through sign and tablet, the first to die—his silence throughout makes his death a kind of punctuation, not pathos. Mira: engineer, competent, lonely, makes a choice in Chapter 4 that she doesn't understand and spends the remaining chapters living inside. **Thematic Anchor:** *We reach toward what we cannot grasp, and the reaching changes us.* Not noble, not tragic. Merely true. --- # Chapter Planning ## Chapter 1: "Something's" **POV:** Mira **Word count target:** ~1000 The hold. Mira completing inventory, the crate unmarked, cold. The ellipsis in her own report to Vance—she hears herself hesitate, hates it. She doesn't say "moving" at first; says "settling," "thermal expansion," lies she doesn't believe. Vance's silence on the comm, then his flat acknowledgment. She stays in the hold after, telling herself she's checking seals. The vibration starts again. She presses her palm to the crate. Not magnetism. Not anything she has words for. The chapter ends with her hand still there, longer than she intended, and her palm afterward carrying a numbness that spreads to the wrist over hours. **Key beats:** The prompt's line delivered with all its hesitation intact. Mira's self-deception. First physical consequence that she doesn't report. ## Chapter 2: "Routine" **POV:** Mira **Word count target:** ~1000 Next day. Mira tries to establish normalcy: breakfast with Doc, who notices nothing; Silas's hands moving through pre-flight checks; Vance absent from the galley, which is usual, but she finds him in the hold, standing too close to the crate, not touching it. He doesn't explain. She doesn't ask. They work in silence. The ship's gravity fluctuates during Silas's burn—minor, within tolerance, but Mira feels it as *direction*, a pull toward the hold that the ship's orientation doesn't explain. She logs it as instrument error. That night, she wakes with her numb hand raised in darkness, fingers moving in patterns she doesn't recognize, not her own volition or not entirely. She forces it still. Doesn't sleep after. **Key beats:** Vance's strange behavior without explanation. Mira's professional suppression of anomaly. The hand's autonomy introduced without drama, almost casually—more disturbing for that. ## Chapter 3: "The Other Ship" **POV:** Mira **Word count target:** ~1000 Proximity alarm. Corporate hunter-killer, Kren's voice on comms demanding the crate by salvage law. Vance refuses with too little force; Mira hears exhaustion, not defiance. The hunter docks. Kren and two boarders come through, professional, armed but not threatening—yet. Vance negotiates. Mira watches the crate from the hold entrance, her position useless if violence starts. The vibration begins during negotiation, audible to everyone. Kren smiles, recognizes something. Offers Vance a price that would clear debts, buy a new ship, a new life. Vance refuses again, and Mira sees why: he's afraid not of losing the crate but of what happens if someone else *takes* it. The boarders leave without violence. Kren's last look at Mira—assessing, not hostile—before the hatch seals. The chapter ends with Mira realizing the vibration stopped exactly when Kren smiled. **Key beats:** External threat without immediate violence. Vance's fear as the true revelation. The entity's response to Kren as unexplained correlation, not communication. ## Chapter 4: "Opening" **POV:** Mira **Word count target:** ~1000 Mira returns to the hold alone. The crate's inspection panel, standard protocol, she tells herself. She opens it. What she sees: not description but *failure of description*—the prose breaks here, fragments, returns. Light that pools wrong. Geometry that hurts. Something that responds to her presence by making the air *heavy* in patterns almost like speech, but the almost is crucial, the failure is crucial. She doesn't offer her hands; she *loses* them, finds them pressed against the interior surface, can't say when she decided. The experience lasts minutes or hours. She emerges to find Doc searching for her. His face when he sees her—she can't read it, won't remember it clearly afterward. What she remembers: the taste of copper, her own voice making sounds that weren't words, and afterward the light under her skin that doesn't wash off, that *moves* when she isn't looking directly at it. **Key beats:** The encounter as cognitively traumatic, not mystical. Doc's reaction as unexplained. Physical transformation without clear meaning. ## Chapter 5: "Kren's Turn" **POV:** Mira **Word count target:** ~1000 Kren's ship doesn't depart. Mira, watching from the bridge, sees them approach the derelict where the crate originated—information Vance withheld, that they must have extracted from ship records. They're searching for something else, another crate or the same phenomenon in different packaging. Silas signs that he's detected their comm traffic, encrypted but directionally clear: they're going to open whatever they find. The chapter cross-cuts between Mira's routine—she tries to work, fails, her hand moving autonomously more often now—and distant observation of Kren's operation. No direct witnessing. Silas reports electromagnetic anomaly. Then silence from Kren's ship. Then automatic distress beacon, then nothing. Vance doesn't celebrate. Doc prays, audibly, which he's never done. Mira feels something she can't name: not satisfaction, not guilt, not even fear. The light under her skin pulses once, synchronized with nothing she can detect. **Key beats:** Parallel destruction without spectacle. The crew's varied responses as character revelation. Mira's emotional flattening as symptom. ## Chapter 6: "Silas" **POV:** Mira **Word count target:** ~1000 Silas dies. Not from external threat—from the ship itself, or from what the ship has become. He's in the pilot's chair when the drive engages without command, a brief burn that snaps his neck against the restraint he hadn't fastened, or fastened incorrectly, or that failed. Mira finds him. The prose here is flat, procedural: the position of the body, the temperature, the signs she checks before accepting. Vance arrives after, his face showing nothing, his hands steady as he helps her move Silas to the medical bay. Doc performs examination, confirms what Mira already knew. They don't discuss what happened. They don't discuss why the drive engaged. The chapter's center is Mira alone with Silas's body before the others arrive, her hand—her changed hand—resting on his chest, the light under her skin visible through his closed eyelids if she looks from certain angles, which she doesn't, not directly. She takes his tablet, his primary communication tool. It doesn't work for her. The chapter ends with her trying to sign his name, failing, her fingers making instead the patterns she woke with in Chapter 2. **Key beats:** Death as stupid accident, not sacrifice. Mira's dissociation. The entity's influence as environmental hazard, not directed malice. ## Chapter 7: "Vance's Choice" **POV:** Mira **Word count target:** ~1000 The *Mourning Star* is failing. Systems engage without command, not dramatically but persistently—lights, recyclers, the drive's low-power maintenance cycle. Vance makes a decision: he's going to vent the hold, the crate, whatever's happening, into the void. Mira finds him at the controls, not secretly; he tells her directly, expects her to help or at least not interfere. She doesn't. She stands in the doorway, her hand raised in that not-herself gesture, and Vance sees something that stops him. Not fear. Recognition, maybe, or its opposite—confirmation of something he already suspected. He asks her one question: "Does it hurt?" She doesn't know how to answer. Yes. No. Not the right question. He doesn't vent the hold. Instead, he goes in himself, alone, closes the door behind him. Mira listens at the hatch. Sounds: his voice, one word she catches, "Ganymede," then silence, then something she can't process as sound, then the hatch releasing from inside. Vance emerges changed in ways she can't specify—older, certainly, but also *smoothed*, as if some tension has left him. He doesn't explain. He goes to his bunk. He doesn't emerge again in this chapter, or possibly ever. **Key beats:** Vance's choice as surrender, not heroism. Mira's witness without understanding. The entity's effect on Vance as parallel to but different from its effect on her—individual variation, not uniform transformation. ## Chapter 8: "Continued" **POV:** Mira **Word count target:** ~1000 After. The *Mourning Star* still functions, barely. Doc remains, tending systems, tending Mira with increasing helplessness. They don't discuss Vance, found in his bunk, dead without visible cause, or with causes too multiple to prioritize. They don't discuss where they're going. Mira pilots now, badly, her hand finding controls through patterns she doesn't learn. The light under her skin has spread, not dramatically, not beautifully, just *there*, a fact she lives with like the numbness, like the wrongness in how she perceives gravity. Doc tries to speak to her about what happened, about what she is now. She can't respond in ways he understands; her speech comes in fragments, technical terms misapplied, occasional sounds that aren't words. He persists. She persists differently. The chapter's final scene: Mira alone on the bridge, the *Mourning Star* drifting, her hand on the console, feeling for vibration. It comes—not from the hold now, from everywhere, the ship itself, the space around it, her own bones. She doesn't smile. She doesn't weep. She notes it in the log, the way she would note any instrument reading: "Movement detected. Continuing." The last words of the story are the first words of the prompt, returned without resolution, the loop unclosed, the condition ongoing. **Key beats:** Survival without triumph. Communication failed and continued in different forms. The prompt's line as ending, not beginning—its meaning transformed by what precedes it, but not explained. Mira's final state as *continued*, nothing more, nothing less.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Mira Chen ## Background Born on the orbital foundries of Leng, where her mother worked die-stamp maintenance until a containment breach took her lungs. Mira was sixteen, already apprenticed to the same line, and learned about death from the company's compensation packet: two months wages, a replacement worker already scheduled. She stayed another six years, long enough to qualify as ship-systems rated, then bought her way onto a freighter heading coreward with money she'd scraped from overtime and a side trade in black-market stimulant precursors. Not addiction—she never used, couldn't afford to—but she understood the market, the desperation, the markup on twelve-hour shifts. She's been on the *Mourning Star* three years. Longer than anywhere since Leng. She doesn't call it home. She calls it "the ship," with a particular flatness that Vance recognizes as attachment. ## Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws **Compulsive measurer.** She knows the ship's dimensions by heart, not approximately: 47.3 meters keel, 12.8 beam, hold volume 340 cubic meters. She times her showers to the second, not from water rationing but from an internal sense that unmeasured time escapes her. When anxious, she calculates: fuel consumption rates, thermal loads, the exact number of rivets in the galley bulkhead (284, she counted during a three-week drift). **Touch avoidant.** Not from trauma specifically, but from Leng's crowded quarters, the constant accidental contact of bodies in industrial space. She flinches from Doc's pats on the shoulder, from Silas's occasional tap to get her attention. The crate's contact in Chapter 1 disturbs her partly because she initiated it, broke her own rule, and can't explain why. **Lies small.** "Fine" when she's not. "Checked that" when she hasn't. The lies are so minor she barely registers them, but they accumulate, a sediment of unacknowledged debt. She told Vance she'd verified the crate's manifest against the derelict's logs. She hadn't. She told Doc she was sleeping better. She hasn't slept through a night in months, since before the crate, since some earlier unmarked point. **Food as texture, not taste.** She eats the same things in the same order: protein paste first, then carbohydrate brick, then the vitamin gel she dislikes and saves for last as punishment. She doesn't notice she's doing this until someone points it out. No one has. ## Physicality Small, 158 cm, which helped in Leng's cramped maintenance shafts and helps still in the *Mourning Star*'s older, non-standard spaces. Hair she cuts herself, unevenly, with a utility knife every few months when it interferes with helmet seal. Hands her most expressive feature: long fingers, always moving, tapping out calculations on invisible surfaces, now increasingly the site of her visible change. She moves economically, no wasted motion, but with a hesitation before entering rooms, a moment of assessment that reads as wariness or politeness depending on observer. Her face in repose seems younger than she is; the lines show only when she speaks, concentrated around her mouth, from holding back or holding in. Post-crate: the light under her skin starts at her left palm, spreads gradually. She wears a glove now, always, which she didn't before. The glove is dirty. She doesn't wash it, doesn't examine what's beneath, not directly. ## Motives, Allegiances, Relationships **To Vance:** complicated debt. He hired her when her qualifications were borderline, her references nonexistent. She suspects he knew about the stimulant precursors, didn't care or cared for the wrong reasons. She performs competence for him, hypervigilance as gratitude. She doesn't know if he notices. **To Doc:** fondness she won't name. His religiosity makes her uncomfortable in ways she hasn't examined—her own parents were company-secular, rituals of production and accident, no vocabulary for his prayers. She finds him after she opens the crate partly because he's who she thinks of when she needs another person present. **To Silas:** functional respect. She doesn't know his history, hasn't asked. His muteness is a relief, no performance of sociability required. His death she processes as system failure: a component that functioned, then didn't. **To herself:** survival as habit, not drive. She doesn't want to die but can't articulate wanting to live. The crate, the change, gives her something she won't call purpose: a condition to observe, a transformation to document even as it consumes her capacity for documentation. ## Voice Samples *"Gravity's off by point three. Not enough to flag. Enough to notice if you're already noticing."* *"I don't dream. That's not—I mean, I don't remember them. The sleep logs show REM. The person in the bed isn't the person who'd remember."* *[After the crate, trying to explain to Doc]* *"It wasn't seeing. It was being seen from. Like the dark had an angle."* --- # Jeb "Ironhand" Vance ## Background The name "Ironhand" came after Ganymede, and he never uses it himself. He was born Jebediah Vance on a failing colony world, no records surviving its abandonment. He enlisted at fifteen, lied about his age, the wars needed bodies. He was good at violence, better at command, survived the Siege of Ganymede as a captain of orbital infantry when survival meant ordering things he still doesn't describe, not even to himself. Post-war: a succession of ships, each smaller, each more marginal. The *Mourning Star* is the last, bought with back-pay and a pension that stopped coming when the corporate administration changed. He drinks rice liquor, the cheap kind that ships to frontier stations because no one else will buy it. He's been sober three times, never longer than a month, doesn't try anymore. He encountered something like the crate before. Not the same. Related. He doesn't know how related, how much his memory is contaminated by what happened after, the medical discharge, the months of treatment he walked out of. He picked up this crate deliberately, tracked its signature, told himself he was curious, knows he was hunting or hunted or both. ## Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws **Performer of competence.** His captaincy is a role he's played so long he no longer distinguishes performance from self. The crew sees steadiness; he experiences it as sustained note held past where breath should fail. He sleeps four hours, medicated, dreams he never reports. **Collector of silences.** He lets conversations die, fills spaces with his presence rather than words. This reads as gravitas, is partly exhaustion, partly calculation. The less he says, the more weight his statements carry. He's aware of this, exploits it, despises himself for exploiting. **Physical cowardice about specific things.** He'll face boarders, vacuum, mechanical failure. He won't enter the medical bay's imaging suite, won't submit to neural scans, won't let Doc examine his hands when the tremor's visible. The tremor comes and goes; he hides it, mostly successfully. **Ritual drinker.** Not continuous, but patterned: one cup upon waking, one before sleep, two at the point in the ship's day when Ganymede would have been visible from the siege positions. He doesn't look at stars during these times. He looks at his hands, the scarred one specifically, a burn pattern whose origin he's forgotten or never knew. ## Physicality Tall, 183 cm, but carries himself smaller, shoulders forward as if approaching a low door. Gray hair he cuts with clippers monthly, no mirror, by feel. The scarred hand: not a prosthetic, despite the nickname's suggestion, but damaged nerves, limited sensation, limited fine motor control. He favors it in ways he doesn't notice, uses it for tasks that don't require precision, saves the good hand for drinking, for keying codes. His face is the product of partial reconstruction, post-Ganymede, and something doesn't move right around his left eye, a slight lag in expression that makes him seem to consider before reacting, which he often is. His smile, rare, arrives too late, outlives the moment. Post-crate encounter: he emerges from the hold in Chapter 7 *smoothed*, as Mira perceives it. The tremor stops. He moves more easily, seems younger. This is not improvement. This is the removal of something that was motivating him, fear or guilt or the particular tension of concealment. ## Motives, Allegiances, Relationships **To Mira:** recognition of something he was or could have been. Her competence, her hidden dealing, her performance of functionality. He hired her partly because she reminded him of himself, partly because he could use her precarity, and he's never sorted the ratio of compassion to exploitation. He doesn't look at her directly after her transformation; not avoidance, but something like respect, or its opposite. **To Doc:** tolerance. Doc's faith is a language Vance doesn't speak, finds slightly embarrassing, like public emotion. He values Doc's medical competence, his steadiness in crisis, finds his prayers before meals intrusive in ways he can't justify. **To Silas:** instrumental. He knows almost nothing about his pilot, hasn't asked. This is a failure he acknowledges without correcting. Silas's death he notes as operational loss, feels something he doesn't examine. **To the crate/entity:** the core relationship he won't articulate. He hunted it, or its kind. He fears it and fears his own desire for it, the way a man might fear returning to a place of trauma. His final entry into the hold is surrender, but to what—redemption, destruction, simple exhaustion—he doesn't specify, and the story doesn't reveal. ## Voice Samples *[To Mira, after her report]* *"Noted."* [Then, after longer silence than the word requires] *"Don't go back in alone."* *[Drinking, to no one, or to the ship]* *"Ganymede was colder. This isn't cold."* *[Final words, if they are words, from inside the hold]* *"You're not the same. I know. I know you're not."* --- # Doc Hollis ## Background Born Eldon Hollis, fourth of seven, on a terraform-station that completed its work and was abandoned, population dispersed, his family to agricultural colonies where the soil was wrong, the light was wrong, where his mother died of something the company doctors called "adjustment syndrome" and his father called grief. He trained as medic through a religious order, the Mendicants of Saint Asphodel, who served frontier stations without corporate charter, surviving on donations and barter. He left the order twelve years ago. Doesn't discuss why. Still prays, still wears the plain silver ring that marks Mendicant service, but the prayers have changed, become private, sometimes wordless. He's been on the *Mourning Star* five years, longer than Mira, longer than Silas. He remembers Vance from before the drinking worsened, from a version of the captain who talked more, smiled more convincingly, and he's not sure if the change is deterioration or revelation of what was always there. ## Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws **Compulsive helper.** He offers treatment before it's requested, advice before it's welcome. This reads as kindness, is partly anxiety management: if he's helping, he's not failing, not standing by as his mother died, as his order dispersed, as the world continues its damage without his intervention. **Religious language without religious certainty.** He prays, but the prayers are increasingly questions, increasingly addressed to absence. He says "God" and means something closer to "pattern" or "possibility," but lacks vocabulary for this shift, so he continues the old forms. **Collector of useless information.** He knows the etymology of medical terms, the history of abandoned colonies, the breeding cycles of station rats. He shares this information at moments of tension, a nervous habit that annoys Vance, comforts Mira in ways she doesn't acknowledge, passes entirely over Silas. **Physical disgust he overrides.** Blood, vomit, the various effluvia of medical practice: he feels revulsion, always has, and performs competence through it. The entity's effects, when they manifest physically in Mira, challenge this managed disgust in ways he doesn't process successfully. ## Physicality Medium height, medium build, the kind of body that disappears in memory, which he cultivated deliberately in his Mendicant years—non-threatening, approachable. Hands that move constantly when he talks, illustrating, emphasizing, sometimes signing unconsciously, fragments of the order's silent service language. He started learning Silas's signing, never completed it, his partial knowledge more awkward than ignorance would be. He smells of the medical bay's antiseptic, always, even when he hasn't been there recently. The smell has permeated his skin. His hair, what's left, he keeps very short; his scalp shows old scars, childhood accidents or adult, he doesn't specify. Post-Mira's transformation: he tries to examine her, fails, tries again. His hands shake when he can't find diagnostic categories for what's happening. This is the first time his competence has failed so completely, and he doesn't have structures for the failure. ## Motives, Allegiances, Relationships **To Vance:** service with reservation. He sees the captain's decline, doesn't intervene effectively, frames this as respect for autonomy but knows it's fear of confrontation, of being dismissed, of losing his place. **To Mira:** protective in ways that embarrass him. She's young enough to be his daughter, though he never had children, never wanted them, and the protectiveness feels borrowed from some script he didn't write. He finds her after the crate opening because he was looking for her, specifically, not because he was checking the hold. **To Silas:** guilt. His partial signing, his failed attempts at communication, his relief that Silas didn't require the emotional labor of spoken interaction. Silas's death frees him from this guilt and adds a worse one. **To the entity:** immediate theological framing, which is his reflex and his limitation. He wants it to be angel or demon, something with intention he can engage. Its actual otherness defeats this framing, leaves him with prayers that don't reach anything he can name. ## Voice Samples *[Finding Mira post-crate]* *"Your hand. Mira. Your hand."* [Then, when she doesn't respond] *"I'm going to touch your shoulder. I'm telling you first. I'm touching your shoulder now."* *[To Vance, about venting the hold]* *"There's a word for what you're describing. It's not mercy.
